
Woman's Exponent.
VOL. 2. 			SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, June 1, 1873.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
			      —
	Texas, also, has been shaken up with an
earthquake.
	The best band for a woman’s  “head’.’—
A reliable hus-band.
	It is held that General Grant is working
fora third term in the White House.  And
if successful, then?
	Pere Hyacinthe has become a father
according to the flesh, Madame Loyson, his
wife having presented him with a son.
And now he preaches against celibacy.
	It is proposed to establish a training
school for nurses in the Connecticut State
hospital, at New Haven and a proposition
so sensible and so novel should succeed.
	It is a penal offense to sell newspapers in
Hamilton, Ohio, on Sunday; but slanderous
gossip is allowed to circulate freely without
punishment. Hamilton is religious—in its
way. 
	The belief that, had Cain been tried be-
fore a modern jury for killing Abel he
would have been acquitted on the ground
of  "emotional insanity,” is fading before
the late executions for murder in different
parts of the United States.
	The Hot Creek band of Modoc Indians
have surrendered, with more “squaws and
papooses” than usually foil to the share of
the same number of male Indians. And
the knowing ones think that Captain Jack
hasonly relieved himselfof non-combatants
to carry on the “war” with increased vigor.
	Dubuque, Iowa, has a couple of girls in
jail for stealing shade trees. The crime is a
peculiar one, and the question arises how
for their love of the beautiful was responsi-
ble for the act? And what a curious
combination of peculiarities might not be
discovered in persons presenting such
diverse characteristics?
	Female emancipation makes wonderful
strides in the republic of Switzerland. At
the last term of Zurich University 110 lady
students were entered and this term 119
are already reported entered with the lists
not closed. The assignment of professional
chairs to women is only considered a matter
of time. And the end is, not yet.  
	The question of George Francis Train’s
sanity or insanity still occupies the attention
of New York judges,and the “next Presi-
dent of America” is confined to a “bastile”
and made to suffer the same penality as if
he was proved guilty of some crime.  It is
surmised that it Train was less wealthy his
sanity would not long trouble the courts.
	“Taxation without representation”
was one great cry in the colonies before the
War of Independence overthrew the British
power. Now the Territories and the
women are taxed without representation.
And the tax-paying women of Rochester,
New York, are organizing and agitating to
discover, if possible, why they should be
subjected to an injustice which the revolu-
tionary fathers preferred to risk life for rather
'than submit to. All success, then, to the
women of Rochester and their example
should be emulated by every tax-paying
woman in the country; and by all the citi-
zens of the Territories, men and women.
[new column]
 “Woman’s Right to work," is a subject
treated upon in a woman’s paper of late
date. Most women have ample opportuni-
ties of gaining practical proof of their
unmitigated right to work, and the neces-
sity of their making full use of that preroga-
tive. The great trouble is with the majority,
they work too much, and think, reason,
contrive and rest too little.
  James Harrison and his daughter, resid-
ing near Walla Walla, Washington Territory,
recently defended themselves against five
Indians, until assistance arrived and cap-
tured three of the Indians. And the world
is united to eulogize them, while the
name of the daughter, who had the courage
and nerve to knock down one of the Indians
with an axe, isn’t even given to the public.
By one man wilfully using powder in the
part of a coal pit where he worked, in Nova
Scotia, about two weeks ago, an explosion of
the gasses in the mine occurred, setting fire
to it and resulting in a horrible death to
fifty-nine men, leaving thirty widows and
ninety-two fatherless children. And such
terrible disasters the result of wilful obsti-
nacy and recklessness, are called “acci-
dents!” 
The ambitious Khedive Of Egypt is
visiting his master, the Sultan of Turkey,
whom he hopes yet to disregard and defy
while the Sultan, who fears and is jealous
of his powerful satrap, receives him with
open arms. All the world is supposed to
know the feelings that exist between them
and all the world may admire in them the
hypocrisy which crowned heads deem a
part of their political education.
The trial of Susan B. Anthony and
fourteen other women, who cast their votes
last Presidential election, and of the inspec-
tor of election who received the votes, is
set for June, to be tried before the Circuit
Court, at Canandaigua, New York. And
the United States, which has raised the late
slaves to the dignity of political sovereignty,
proposes to punish, as criminals, a few of
the most intelligent women in the country
for exercising the right of political citizen-
ship.
A Young woman in one of the govern-
ment departments in Washington was
detected “in conduct unbecoming an offi-
cer,” and was dismissed. In other words
she had been seduced, and her sin was found
out. But her seducer was, apparently, not
guilty of “conduct unbecoming an officer,”
as he was not dismissed. He remains in
his position, receives the public money,
enters society, can be guilty frequently of
immorality, may boast of it, nay may drag
other victims down to shame, and yet
nothing of all this is “conduct unbecoming
an officer”—in him. And why is this so?
And wherefore should he, because he is a
man, be free to enact, unharmed, his wick-
edness and immorality? Why should he be
supported with government money in his
crimes, and have an impunity to sin
more than the woman who has fallen
victim to his arts, in the immediate employ-
ment of the “best government under the
sun?” Here is work for the reformers in high
places, if they are honest and serious; here
is a chance to show that it is not sex, nor
[new column]
ondition, but purity and worth that shouId
eceive pay, for this matter is no secret, and
it has called forth numerous strictures in
Washington society. But still the “young
man” remains a public servant; and the
“young woman” becomes a castaway!
  Among the signs of the times we note a
fearful tornado that swept over Washington
County, Iowa, on the 23rd ult., perhaps the
most terrible ever reported on the continent.
The roaring of the tornado is reported to
have been appalling six miles from its track,
and was heard fully ten miles away.
The following is taken from the descrip-
tion of it, as reported at the time by tele-
graph: “The tornado was about a half a mile
in width and tore into fragments every-
thing that came in its course. Houses,
barns, fences, trees, cattle and human beings
were caught up and whirled through the
air like mere toys and then dashed to the
ground with such violence as to produce
instant death. Houses and barns were torn
in fragments and scattered for miles around.
The fields are dotted with large timbers
driven into the ground at an angle of ninety
degrees, and cattle were actually driven
head foremost into the ground. One can
scarce conceive of the devastation or realize
the force of the tornado. Already we have
heard of thirteen farm houses and many
barns that were literally torn to pieces and
many others that were badly damaged. A
school house, six miles north of here was
torn to pieces and a fourteen-year old
daughter of Henry Rathnel was carried
about a quarter of a mile from the school
house and dashed to the ground and crushed
to a jelly. Miss Smith, the teacher, and
six or eight scholars were hurt, some badly.
Mrs. Henry Watters was killed. Miss
Gardner and a son of Alexander Gibson Ho
at the point of death from their injuries.
A gentleman who was near the tornado
when it passed, reports that it was balloon-
shaped with the small end to the ground and
moved at the rate of about twenty miles an
hour. It commenced its fearful work near
Keoka, where it demolished several resi-
dences. We next hear of it about six miles
northeast, where it destroyed two or three
houses, and as it passed on northeast it
scattered to the four winds the fine resi-
dences and barns of Alex. Gibson, J. A.
Babcock, and J. C. Culingbone. It also
leveled to the ground the dwellings of D.
Caucorf and H. Walters. Mr. Carringer and
Mr. Kerr and many persons lives were
saved by hastily getting into their cellars:
Sad havoc was made with all kinds of stock.
An eye witness says he could she large
pieces of timber hurled from the cloud as
though shot from a cannon. Hail felt that
measured nine inches In diameter. Some
were brought to this city and four hours
after they were picked up,- they were still
as large as hen’s eggs. A telegram from
Keoka last evening says five persons were
killed about three miles from that place
and one child was torn to pieces.” This is
the warning of storm and tempest, which
has been promised to succeed the solemn
testimony of God’s judgments at hand in
these latter days; but the people pay little
more heed to the terrible character, of. the
one, than they did to the gentle and heavenly
natfire of the other.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


AN ACROSTIC.
				——
When women unite and each other invite,
"Oh come and subscribe for our paper."
May a blessing attend upon all who defend,
And help us to prai e our Creator;
Now, we are but a few yet we great things can do,
Sisters come join heart and hand,
Every one take delight and contribute her might, the
'XPONENT to send through the land
People all the world round think us trodden down,
Oppress'd and degraded, but know,
No existence as yet out of our Deseret
E'er the blessings of peace could bestow;
Nothing here can compare with our husbands, to
	share
The great gifts eternal, began here below.
							MRS. E. L. Hyde,
13TH WARD, Salt Lake City.


﻿MY CHOICE.
			      ——
		BY JENNIE HARRISON.
			       ——
Yes; I know there are stains on my carpet—
    The traces of small muddy boots;
And I see your fair tapestry glowing
    All spotless with blossoms and fruits!

And I know that my wal's are disfigured
    With prints of small fingers and hands;
And I see that your own household whiteness
    All fresh in its purity stands.

Yes, I know my "black walnut” is battered,
    And dented by many small heels;
While your own polished stairway, all perfect,
    Its smooth shining surface reveals !

And I know that my parlor is littered
    With many odd treasures and toys;
While your own is in daintiest order, 
    Unharmed by the presence of boys!

And I know that my room is invaded
    Quite boldly all hours of the day;
While you sit in your own unmolested
    And dream the soft quiet away !

Yes, I know I have jacke's that wear out,
    And buttons that never will stay;
While you can embroider at leasure.
    And learn pretty arts of "crochet.”

And I know there are lessons in spelling,
    Which I must be patient to hear;
While you may sit down to your novel,
    Or turn the last magazine near.

Yes, I know there are four little bedsteads 
   Where I must stand watchful each night;
While you may go out in your carriage,
    And flash in your dresses so bright !

Now, I think I’m a neat little woman—
    I like my house orderly, too;
And I’m fond of all dainty belongings—
    Yet I would not change places with you !

No! keep your fair home, with its order,
    Its freedom from trouble and noise;
And keep your own fanciful leisure;
    But give me my four splendid boys !


R.S. REPORTS.
			—
		Richfield, May 11th, 1873.
Dear Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
 I deem it a privilege and a duty to write
a few lines for your interesting paper.  I
have an earnest desire to assist in rolling
forth the work in which we are engaged in
these the last days. We have a Relief
Society organized here in Richfield, which
is doing much good in helping the poor,
both at home and abroad. In our last
monthly meeting, May 1st, we agreed to
omit the word “Female” from the name of
our Society. The sisters, in general, feel
well in their religion; and are well paid for
taking your paper; much information is
gained there, from.
  May we ever increase in the Truth.
	Respectfully yours in the Gospel,
		Eliza A. Ogilvie, President.
			 —
 From Providence, Cache County, April
29th, 1873: The Relief Society of this
place, held its annual meeting in the school-
[new column]
room, on Monday, 28th inst. Owing to a
snow storm prevailing that day the meeting
was slimly attended. The Secretary read
the income and expenditure for the past
year; which showed a balance of $103:23 in
favor of the Society. The sisters present
bore faithful testimonies to the good which
had been effected through their weak instru-
mentality also expressed united desires to
support and strengthen, by their labor and
means and further extend the usefulness
of the thriving institution. A few of the
brethren followed with congratulatory and
encouraging remarks, which brought the
meeting to a close, every one enjoying a
fulness of the Spirit of God. We thank-
fully receive the Exponent which is much
appreciated.
	Mrs. Margaret Mathews, Prest.
			    —
 The Relief Society of Ogden met in the
new city hall, May 1st, 1873, to engage in a
meeting and “Pic nic.”.
 President Mrs. J. S. Richards called .the
meeting to order:  Singing:  Prayer by
Secretary Mrs. A. M. Frodsham: Singing:
Minutes of last meeting read and accepted.
President Mrs. J. S. Richards remarked
that she had invited the young ladies from
the schools to come to meeting: President
Young had given a great deal of good in-
struction to the sisters during Conference;
he wished them to save all their paper
rags, thus assisting to commence measures
for making their own paper and books. She
liked the Exponent very much and hoped
the sisters would all try to take it. Sister
Belknapp, President of the Relief Society,
at Hoopersville, and sister Higbee from
Salt Lake City, made appropriate remarks.
  Elder F. D. Richards felt truly thankful
to see so many of the sisters together; hoped
the society would continue to increase until
every good sister was united with it. Be-
sides blessing the poor, one great object
of the society was that the daughters of
Zion might learn their duties as mothers
and towards each other; treasuring up every
thing that was good, and overcoming all
that was evil. Life's labors were life’s
pleasures.  Brother Phineas Richards gave
excellent instructions in regard to bringing
up children and how they should be dressed.
Advised the sisters to search the Sciptures
and learn to understand truth from error for
themselves. Sister Wordsworth next ad-
dressed the meeting, and was followed by
sister Petry, who said that the next Sunday
she would be eighty-three years old, and
she thanked God that she had embraced the
Gospel.   Sister Elizabeth Browning and
sister Hadlock made remarks. President
Mrs. J. S. Richards felt blessed in listening
to the testimonies of father Phineas Rich-
ards, eighty-five, and mother Petry, eighty-
three years old; it was edifying and
gratifying to listen to such aged people.
Remarks were made by sister Sarah Nelson
and Secretary A. M. Frodsham. Sister J.
S.Richards then appointed a meeting for
the young ladies on the following Thursday
at 4 o’clock p. m. After which, refresh-
ments were partaken of; and of about 140 per-
sons who were present, there was none who
did not appear to enjoy the good spirit and
feel satisfied with the way in which the
afternoon was spent.
  Meeting adjourned and was dismissed
with singing, and prayer by sister Belknapp.
  According to appointment a meeting of
the young ladies of Ogden was held on the
8th of May, at which much valuable counsel
and instruction was given by President Mrs.
J. S. Richards and others. Eighty young
ladies were present and all, by a unani-
[new column]
mous vote, expressed themselves pleased
with the meeting. . Another was appointed
for the following Wednesday at half past 4
p. m.
			A. M. Frodsham, Secretary.
				 —
 At the 58th meeting of the Senior and
Junior Co-operative Retrenchment Associa-
tions of this city, held May 24th, 1873, in
the 14th Ward Assembly Rooms, simple
medicines, how to gain health and how to
preserve it were the principal topics treated
upon. Counselor Mrs. Z. D. Young made
appropriate remarks; Counselor Mrs. S. M.
Kimball gave an intelligent and highly
edifying address on the above mentioned
subjects and Mrs. Watmough related some
facts proven by experience, etc.  The same
subjects were to be continued at the next
meeting.


LETTER FROM ST. GEORGE.
				   ——
		    ST. GEORGE, UTAH, May 13, 1873.
DEAR EXPONENT:—
    Perhaps you thought “Flora” had faded
out, like a withered rose, or gone to seed
like a dandelion. No such thing; not a bit
of it. But the household pets, both animate
and inanimate, or the children and flower
nursing, after the thousand duties of the
morning are over, leave the maternal head
of the family little time for "pen and ink
sketches." Now you wonder what a woman
can find to busy herself about so much in
this pleasant, sunny clime, where the “shade
of the vine and fig tree" is supposed to yield
a tranquil and safe repose; but my dear
woman, though the fact is here, the idea is
a myth. The vine and fig tree already
furnish a delicious and fragrant shade, to
woo us on to its enjoyment—but—ah that
treacherous word—how often it steps in to
mar our brightest hopes and expectations of
happiness and delight! Just as we step
forward to enter upon the sweet enjoyment,
we remember that the verdant vine and fig
tree, as well as the little ones, must be fed,
and that we have omitted to give them
their moisture in season—yes, and all the
other pets in the parlor garden await their
period of nursing ere their fragrant bloom
droops. So with a hearty pleasure we turn
aside from the coveted ease, and live with
our flowers for an hour; studying and sup-
plying their wants, bringing a pleasure and
giving a rest that makes the hour seem but
a moment. By this time we become so
much interested that we fill the remaining
leisure by a stroll around the flower garden,
where the rose, the lily and the peonia
in wondrous beauty and gorgeous array
hold queenly court amid the thousand lesser
flowers, which, in hues and forms innumer-
able, adorn our little Eden, and are widely
scattered along the terraced walks; where
the musical waterfalls sparkle like diamonds
in the sunlight.
    We often wonder that our sisters do not
more adorn their houses and their gardens
with flowers to gladden the hearts of the
household, yielding a power that enchains
each to the home-hearth, and with an unseen
influence, bringing love and refinement,
kindness, peace and joy to the home circle.
Sisters, let the bloom of flowers surround
you, and you will think less of repose under
the vine and fig tree. Ever thine
							FLORA.


ENGLISH PAPERS say that the Latter-day
Saints in Manchester Conference refused
to believe that President Brigham Young
had resigned, and held that the statement
to that effect was a newspaper slander.
And the Manchester Saints were correct.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

        A GOOD wife, a true woman is a real
heroine and an accomplished actress. She
puts her own grievances out of sight to
drive away with pleasant smiles the clouds
that gather around her husband's gloomy
brow; she pours oil on the troubled waters
of her own soul, that she may sooth his
sorrow; she dashes the teardrop from her
eye that she may remove the cup of bitter-
ness from his lips, while he suspects not
there are sorrows weighing down her
aching heart. She cultivates cheerfulness,
and endeavors to banish despondency, for
she is a true philosopher, and knows that a
gloomy spirit but aggravates the ills of life.
    Perhaps if husbands were more considerate
and good wives better appreciated, happy
homes would be more numerous. But many
a good wife goes down to death and they
raise not a stone, carve not a line to tell of
her many virtues, but as she fades from
earth her self-sacrificing, noble nature fades
also from the memory of the living. She
does not seek her reward in the world's
praises, neither is it to be found there, but
if her duties are rightly and well performed,
her reward will surely come.
    A HOME OF TASTE.—Every good picture
is the best of sermons and lectures. The
sense informs the soul. Whatever you have
have beauty. Let beauty be on the paper on
your walls. It is as easy to choose a paper
suggestive of the lovely in color and form,
as the uncouth. Why should not every
household object be sanctified with this
grateful charm? Each chair, each table,
each tea or chamber-service, and every
object for kitchen or parlor, for the home of
the poor man, artizan or mechanic, I would
have them all worthy of a home of taste.—
"Sydney Smith."
    THE scientific branch of cookery com-
prises the devising of dishes and sauces. The
artistic branch constitutes the art of garnish-
ing, and this plays a most important part in
the outcome of the kitchen, as by means of
it, dishes please the eye before they please
the palate. First impressions go a great
way, and when one sense is captivated by
an agreeable and inviting appearance, the
dish must be bad indeed which fails to stand
the more searching ordeal of taste. Besides,
people who suffer from jaded appetites have
a better chance of eating their dinner when
the dishes which are put before them are
pleasant to the sight.
    ALL green vegetables of the cabbage kind
should be chosen with large, close, firm
hearts; when fresh the leaves are crisp and
brittle; when stale they are lank and droop-
ing.
    PUDDING cloths, however coarse, ought
never to be washed with soap; they should
be dried as quickly as possible, and kept
free from dust, and in a drawer or cupboard
free from smell.
    A GOOD lip-salve, useful for chaps, etc.,
is made of equal parts of almond or olive
oil, and the best white wax: melt the latter
in a clean gallipot, set at the side of the fire,
then add the oil.
    GERMAN FRITTERS.—Cut some slices of
bread half an inch thick, remove the crust,
and soak in milk: beat two eggs, pour them
over the bread, and fry it in butter; strew
powdered sugar over the fritters.
    ITALIAN RICE PUDDING.—A teacup of
rice, the yolks of four eggs, the whites of
three beaten separately, two ounces of
pounded sugar, two ounces of raisins, a
quarter pound of suet chopped very fine,
flavoring of ratafia or vanilla. Put these in-
[new column]
gredients into a mould, boil, and serve
with brandy sauce.
    PARSNIP SOUP.—One pound of sliced
parsnips, two ounces of butter, salt and
cayenne to taste, one quart of stock. Put
the parsnips into the stewpan with the but-
ter, which has previously melted, and
simmer them till quite tender. Then add
nearly a pint of stock, and boil together for
half an hour. Pass all through a fine
strainer, and put to it the remainder of the
stock. Season, boil, and serve immediately.
    A SURE CURE FOR COLD.—New-laid egg,
well-beaten, dessertspoonful of fine oatmeal,
dessertspoonful of moist sugar, a little
powdered ginger, little salt, half an
ounce of fresh butter. All mixed well to-
gether. Pour on  half a pint, or rather more,
of boiling water, gently stirring quickly all
the time to prevent curdling. To be taken
at bedtime until the cold is removed.
    STEWED RED CABBAGE.—One red cab-
bage, a small slice of ham, half an ounce of
fresh butter, one pint of stock or broth, one
gill of vinegar, salt and pepper to taste, one
tablespoonful of pounded sugar. Cut the
cabbage into very thin slices, put it into a
stewpan, the ham cut in dice, the butter,
half a pint of stock, and the vinegar: cover
the pan closely, and let it stew. When it is
very tender, add the remainder of the stock,
a seasoning of salt and pepper, and the
pounded sugar: mix all well together, stir
over the fire until all the liquor is dried
away, and serve. Fried sausages are
usually sent to table with this dish; they
should be laid around and on the cabbage,
as a garnish.


LETTER TO THE RELIEF SOCIETY.
			——
    My Dear Sisters: Holy is the work to
which you are called, and blessed is she who
performs it nobly. Blessed be she who adds
even one drop of benevolence and good-will
to the crystal fountain. And blessed be she
whose heart fails not in the cause, and who
withholds not the little she can do, because
she cannot do great things. Think not, be-
cause your time and means are limited, you
can  do nothing to forward the work. If it
be really impossible (which can hardly be
the case,) for you to give either money or
handiwork,  you can, if you will, give a
steadfast faith, a warm enthusiasm, a
cheerful, kindly word, a generous appreci-
ation of and pride in what others accom-
plish and thus help to encourage and
stimulate those who can and will support
the cause.
    If you have no poor in your neighborhood,
(and pray God you have not,) there are hun-
dreds of other objects worthy to receive the
benefit of your labors. First of all is the
comforting of each other. For this purpose,
meet often together and speak to each other;
exchange your ideas; give yours and receive
others, thus blessing and being blessed. If you
should not absolutely learn anything new,
you may have your mind refreshed concern-
ing something old which you would not
like to forget; or some word you hear may
set in motion a train of new and profitable
reflections.
    Be not afraid of the sound of your own
voices; if your diffidence drive ideas from
your mind the first time you rise, promise
yourself you will have your nerves and
thoughts under better control another time,
and do not put that other time off too long.
If you think others are older and wiser than
you, so be it; but the one truth you may be
able to express is just as true as any one of
the ten another may set forth, and should
not be concealed because it is but one.
Love and uphold each other; strengthen
[new column]
each other's faith and remembrance; and
both by precept and example help to elevate
the moral standard of your community.
    Woman's mission is especially to the
young; and therein, (Oh, could she only
realize it!) she rules the world. The hopes
and aspirations of the women of this genera-
tion, become the constitutional disposition
of the next; so any measure, moral, religious,
political, social or domestic which is uni-
versally desired by mothers, their children
in after years will surely accomplish in spite
of opposition. Then let "us," at least, fix
our desires on those things which are holy
and elevating in their influence. Let us
not harbor any harmful or ignoble thoughts.
Bear with each other's failings and weak-
nesses; build up each other's reputation
and strengthen each other's hands. Let
not a whisper of jealousy enter your hearts
or pass your lips. If some one advances
more good suggestions than you can, envy
not her talents but diligently improve your
own. If one occupying a superior position
frowns down your suggestions, murmur
not; if she adopt them for her own, leaving
you unknown in the matter, let it rest be-
tween her and her conscience; and feel
cause for grateful pleasure in the fact that
the plan was a good one, no matter where
originated. If you do not receive  your
dues of appreciation personally for the good
you do, remember any good accomplished
by woman in general reflects credit on each
individual woman; and in this important
fact find abundant reward.
    Happily few are so poor that, if they love
the cause, they cannot contribute a little to
the funds; and it is by the union of many
littles that great things are achieved. Sup-
pose each member of a society should give
monthly a small stated sum into the
treasury; say fifty cents, twenty-five cents,
or even ten cents, it would be a very light
burden individually, if given with a grate-
ful heart, and it would accumulate surpris-ingly. Very soon, a few choice volumes
could be laid on the shelves for the use of
the sisterhood, and much valuable instruc-
tion be gained by appointing one to read
aloud while others sew. A sewing or
knitting machine would add much to your
resources; and I think you are the proper
persons to keep burial clothes on hand to be
had at an hour's warning.
    Who knows but you might be able to give
a handsome present to the Temple or emi-
grating funds, or other public benefactions
which are dear to your hearts? And how
nice it would be to build and furnish for
yourselves large commodious rooms in which
to hold your working meetings, your prayer
meetings and your business meetings; or to
assemble your daughters for instruction in
the ways you love to have them walk in.
No one can venture to foretell half the good
a regular though small income might enable
you to accomplish.
    It is worthy of consideration, sisters, and
I beg you will not hastily decide that you
cannot afford it, for you do afford many a
thing less desirable. Say you will afford it
for the sake of God and womanhood; and
then leave off the ruffles from Mary's dress
and the braid from Johnny's jacket, and thus
you not only save a little money but a little
time, a little strength, a little patience also;
and trust me, you will soon feel well paid.
    In conclusion, bear in mind that any
organization requires time to grow mature
by experience; so be not discouraged if you
make some mistakes. Through prayer and
diligence keep the Spirit of God, and all your
undertakings will be prospered. That such
may be our happy lot is the prayer of your
humble sister,				L. L. D.

  Whitelaw Reid denies his intention to
marry Anna Dickinson......The editress of
“Hearth and Home,” Mrs. Mary Dodge, is
going abroad for her health......Kate Stan-
ton is lecturing on the “Loves of Great
Men.”......The minister of the interior of
France and the minister of public instruc-
tion have tendered their resignations be-
cause they cannot serve in the same cabinet.
......A young man in New York lately killed
three bears with a nail hatchet......Japan
shows a growing interest in the use of the
English language, sixty copies of Webster’s
Unabridged dictionary were lately shipped
for Yokahoma......Professor Agassiz com-
pleted his sixty-sixth year on the 28th of
May. he has been in America 27 years.
......It is haying time now in Berryessa
valley......St. Albans, Vermont, has had a
$150,000 fire......A terrible fire in Drum-
mond colliery, near Picton, Nova Scotia,
was lately occasioned by an explosion of gas.
......The total vote of Spain at the last
election was for a constituent Cortes
and numbered 1,250,000......On the same
day recently one murderer was hung at Mt.
Carroll, Illinois, and another in New York.
......Three thousand Bibles are claimed to
be daily distributed by the American Bible
Society......There are 8,403 places in New
York City where liquor is sold......The Pit
River Indians are trying to bring about a
war with the white settlers there, though
their regular chief is for peace......Boss
Tweed, it is reported, pleads “not guilty”
on fifteen indictments for fraud......Vien-
nese Commissioner, General Van Buren,

Senor Castelar’s work on Rome into English.

from Iowa to Kansas......Italy has seven-
teen universities attended by 4,204 students.

has been appropriated by the Illinois Legis-
lature......Yale College as a petrified Indian,
discovered and dug up last Summer on the
bluffs of the North Platt......one hundred
thousand valuable specimens in mineralogy,

lected by Professor Agassiz, and party......
Tea is proposed as a substitute for brandy
in the Russian army they will, doubtless,
learn in time that pure water, undefiled
by either is the most wholesome beverage.
......Mules are exempted by the Georgia
Society for the prevention of cruelty to
animals....One of the earliest and best
woman preachers, Olympia Brown, was
lately married at Providence. R. I.......
From all parts of the country come good
reports of the appearance of the wheat crops
this season......A man in Minnesota was
found dead with his head in a kettle of
water the other morning......A writer from
San Diego says positively the people there
live on climate; it would be a nice thing to
have such a climate broadly extended......

for a heroine......Sumner County, Kansas,

year......Philadelphia ladies are petitioning
for female inspectors of woman prisoners......

this country......The expense of the Euro-
pean tour of the Shah of Persia is estimated
at L5,000,000......The daughter of Ben Hol-

on the 16th ult......Dental surgery has been

women in New York......Three wives of the
Shah of Persia travel with him......The 16th
of May this year, was celebrated by the

with four legs and four wings, but only one
head......Elizabeth Cady Stanton wants

“give in and let the women vote.”......The

of oysters during the Summer months......

greater and better amount of rational teach-
ing done by the female teachers in Boston
primary schools than by the professors of
Harvard College


VOLUME TWO.
					  ——
    The EXPONENT to-day enters its second year.
While its success has exceeded the expecetations
entertained when its publication was com-
menced, it has not been such as to yet justify
certain improvements which we hope at an
early day to introduce. The question of en-
largement, or of issuing weekly instead of
bi-weekly, has been considered, but it requires
even a larger subscription list than we yet
have to warrant such a step. We have not
sought to make the EXPONENT an advertising
medium, although advertisements are the great
source of income for most journals; the desire
being to devote the space to matter of greater
importance to our readers. And while the
business was indeed new to us a year ago, we
have received kind and freely tendered assist-
ance, which has been much appreciated, and
have gained experience during the year, that
will aid us in future editorial labors. It is not
to be supposed that in the limited space at
our command there could be given all the
variety of topics and reading matter which a
larger journal contains; but our efforts have
been directed towards imparting as much and
varied information as possible, on topics of
interest to women generally. The many
cheering letters continually received from all
parts of the Territory, and from other parts of
the world, have been encouraging, and have
given assurance that those efforts have not
been altogether unsuccessful.
    In commencing the second year of the EXPO-


NENT we earnestly desire a continuation of the
aid so cheerfully tendered in the past; contri-
butions on such topics of interest as our lady
correspondents may feel led to write upon;
reports, as before, of the works and progress of
the many Relief Societies of the Territory,
which, to our mind, has been one of the most
interesting features of the last volume; and such
other assistance as a desire to foster and extend
the influence of the journal for good may
suggest. And we shall endeavor in diligence,
in faithfulness, and with humble reliance on
the Spirit of the Almighty, to so conduct it,
and fill its pages, that it may be and continue
to be truly the exponent of the women of Utah
and become a necessity in every household
where right and truth are the governing prin-
ciples.


﻿HINTS ON HOME LIFE.
	
	It is never wise to make any sudden change
in modes of living. If one extreme has been
followed, in correcting the mistakes modera-
tion should be studied, and great care be taken
to avoid the very frequent error straightway
adopting the opposite extreme, which, in many
cases, may prove to be as bad, or worse than
the former. For instance, if a person has fol-
lowed the practice of bathing in warm or tepid
water, and no oftener than once a week, it
would not be wisdom to begin at once taking
daily baths in cold water, although it is now
generally conceded that the latter form of
bathing is a most healthy one.
	Homes where love and cheerfulness abound,
are the homes of industry and frugality. There
may be a seeming ecstacy in luxury accom-
panied with leisure and indolence; but the true
joys of home-life exist in the care and pains
taken by all the members of a family, and the
labors performed by each, in the creation of
sunshine and happiness.
	It should not be considered burdensome and
trying to perform any domestic duty, or make
any sacrifice which reason demands, to promote
the welfare and satisfaction of any or all por-
tions of the household. Children are very apt
to dislike work, and to complain of the tasks
required of them, even though they are light
and trifling. This is not because employment
of head and hands does not accord with the
laws of nature. Infants prove by their ever
busy, restless movements in their wakeful
moments, that action is an essential of ani-
mated being; and needs only to be properly
governed and trained that it may move in the
channel of usefulness. Parents often give their
children incorrect ideas concerning the use of
this all important power without intending to
do so. While verbally seeking to instill into
the youthful mind the divine principle
that "there is no excellence without labor,"
they so far forget themselves as to utter com-
plaints in the presence of their children, of
their own hard fare; and by whining over the
arduous labors they have to perform, the exer-
tions they are obliged to make, and the weari-
some life assigned them, they lead the little
people to think that work is merely a drag
which should be kept off if possible.
	If a mother gives vent to cheerless and disturbed
feelings about her duties, the work which she
feels unable to do, instead of doing what she
can with pleasure and fortitude, making fore-
thought and prudence do their great part in
assisting, being never idle, yet uncomplaining
over what she cannot accomplish, her little
daughters will grow to believe that the portion
of work allotted to them is too much, and that
they are justified in finding fault. Thus the
whole household gets into confusion, and lack


of system renders domestic duties tedious and
troublesome. But the opposite course being
taken by the mother—the superintendent and
manager of the whole—will ensure general
satisfaction and pleasure in all the rounds of
domestic life. Let parents show that they de-
light, as they should do, in the necessary duties
which they perform and the same spirit will be
diffused through the whole family circle and
life’s labors will be accomplished in blissful
harmony, while peace and prosperity and joy
will be gathered as the golden grain and deli-
cious fruits.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE "WOMAN QUESTION."

	Perhaps no agitation looking, to the accom-
plishment of a great result ever progressed so
rapidly in the time, or at the same stage had
brighter prospects of success, than that which
seeks to secure for women the right to enjoy
and exercise the elective franchise, and the
right to earn her living by any means of in-
dustry open to man for which she has capacity.
In some of the States of this Republic—notably
Massachusetts—where high-sounding promises
had been made, the agitation has appeared to
be retrograding instead of advancing; yet while
this has been the case in one or two places, the
progress in other parts of the world has been
rapid and marked.  From Turkey comes in-
formation that a great innovation has been made
on venerable custom, and that even ladies of
higest rank are proposing to discard the great
vails which have been used for long centuries
to cover woman’s features, so that no man’s
gaze might rest upon them unless that of the
husband. This would be as much of a step in
advance there as for the women of the United
States to be placed in possession of the elective
franchise.  In England, where there is the ex-
ample of a woman for chief ruler of that mighty
power, the advance towards woman suffrage
has equalled, if not surpassed, the warmest
expectations of those engaged in the agitation.
A measure introduced in the House of Com-
mons, by Mr. Jacob Bright, to extend the
suffrage to women, was lately defeated, yet it
is only a few years ago since the proposition of
such a measure in the British Parliament
would have been sneered down, and been met
with nothing stronger than ridicule and con-
tempt. The fact that it can now be introduced
and calmly discussed shows an unexpected
change of sentiment that indicates success at a
not very remote day. And throughout Eng-
land, at vestry meetings and pa ochial elections,
the right of tax-paying women to speak and
vote is recognized. Switzerland is breaking
down the barriers which prohibited women
from obtaining education at her leading sem-
inaries.  While, under the protecting care of
the Crown Princess of Germany, Queen Vic-
toria’s daughter, woman’s advancement in
many ways is being widely encouraged and
forwarded. In the United States the agitation
has been seriously injured by the extreme
views urged by certain advocates of woman’s
rights views that, if widely entertained and
practised, would uproot society, bring social
chaos, and introduce another cycle of “dark
ages.” Every argument which aims at divid-
ing the interests of the sexes, is a blow to
human progress, weak or powerful as the case
may be.  And while woman should have and
enjoy every right which her Creator has de-
signed for her. She never can form nor have a
world of her own, where “horrid man” will
not be found. The “Woman Question” is the
question of both man and woman and “wo-
man’s rights” should as deeply interest men as
women. Woman is the mother of the world,
[new column]
and her interests can no more be separated from
those of man, than could the world exist with
only one sex. It is to be hoped that this fact
will have full weight in the future agitation of
the “Woman Question” and if it has, and the
extreme and revolutionary theories of a few
are discarded, it will be but a few years until
the desired political right, and every other
legitimate one associated with it, will be en-
joyed by women throughout the United States;
which should be in advance of Britain and
not behind it in such a matter of human pro-
gress.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.
				 —
 Two more of those interesting letters from
Miss Eliza R. Snow will be found in the
present number of the EXPONENT.
    THE PALESTINE Party entirely missed the
late Winter of ’72-3, their continuous route
bringing them to each point which they visited
just as merry Spring approached.  Mr. Little
says he has no recollection of having seen a
particle of ice since the close of the previous
Winter.
   FOURTEEN DAYS of almost constant rain is
something so remarkable in the history of
Utah, since its settlement, that it is question-
able if any previous year has witnessed it.
Yet that is something like what we have had
during the month of May, in the present year;
and it has been a climatic phenomenon worth
remembering.
   THE LATEST news received from those of the
Palestine party now in Europe was in a short
telegram which appeared in the daily papers
last week, stating that President George A.
Smith had been present at a conference of the
Saints held in London, on the Sunday pre-
vious, May 25th.  Elder F. Little and Miss
Claire Little, of the party, returned home since
the last number of the EXPONENT was issued
arriving on the 21st ult. in good health, and
accompanied from Europe by Mrs. Little who
had crossed the Atlantic to meet them.
    TO-DAY, the seventy-third birthday of Presi-
dent Brigham Young, finds that honored
member of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints, still active and energetic in
the cause of Truth, for which he has labored so
exclusively for more than forty years.  For
one of his age, his health is very excellent; he is
still able to speak in his natural, clear and
impressive voice to the entire satisfaction of
large congregations; and he still, continually
and most deservedly, enjoys the sincere faith
and confidence of the people to whom he has
long since fully proven himself a trust-worthy
friend, servant and counselor.
   REPORTS FROM Mrs. A. M. Frodsham, Secre-
tary of the Relief Society of Ogden, shows that
the young ladies of that city have been organ-
ized into an association, and hold meetings
Semi-monthly, for the purpose of assisting the
Relief Society in its charitable labors, and also
to gain wisdom from instructions pertaining to
the Gospel of Christ and all useful branches of
practical education.  Seventy members have
already been admitted to this Society of young
ladies. We wish them all success, and grate-
fully acknowledge a special and very cordial
invitation from them to attend their next
meeting, to be held in the new city hall,
Ogden, on Thursday, 5th inst., at 4 o’clock in
the afternoon.


A NOVEL by the Princess Mathilde Bona-
parte, entitled "La Dame a la Rubine," will
shortly be published. This novel, it is said,
was laid before M. Prosper Merimee, some
time before his decease, and was corrected
by him.


WHAT NOT.
			       ——
    THE figure 9 is said to be like a pea-
cock, because it is  0 without its tail.
    A TALL and beautiful lady is described as
"lengthened sweetness long drawn out."
    WHEN a girl falls in love with an unlucky
Irishman, her heart always goes pity-Pat.
    THE gentleman who lately took the fresh
air in the Central Park is requested to re-
store it.
    THE highest office within the gift of the
people is the weather signal station on
Pike's Peak.
    "I'm going to draw this beau into a
knot," as the lady said when standing at
the Hymeneal altar.
    A TOURIST who was asked in what part
of Switzerland he felt the heat most, replied
"When I was going to Berne."
    NEXT to a new bonnet, a black moustache
has been known to do the most violence to
harmony among young ladies.
    IT is said that the finest High School
building in the United States is at Omaha,
Nebraska. Cost, $250,000.
    A SUCCESSFUL female barber in Phila-
delphia shows what woman may do to
"razor" self to independence.
    ONE Missouri editor says of another that
"his ears would do for awnings for a ten-
story wholesale hog-packing establishment."
    A YALE College student said he had
"rather be boss pancake baker in Vassar
College, than to have Professor Tyndall's
head on his shoulders."
    THE saloon-keepers of Big Rapids re-
solved to close their saloons and kill their
town if the council cidn't reduce their
license from $500 to $300. The council
didn't.
    COMPOSITORS often put in queer things
"to fill out a column." Here is the latest:
"Does not a young mother's heart leap 4th
with joy when she beholds her darling
babe's 1st 2th.
    "You had better ask for manners than
money," said a finely dressed gentleman to
a beggar who asked for alms. "I asked
you for what I thought you had the most
of," was the reply.
    A 'FRISCO man runs a partnership busi-
ness alone, being both pound-master and
sausage dealer. He killed 221 dogs in
April, and  people wonder why they got
their best links so late in the spring.
    WHITELAW Reid, editor of the New
York Tribune, gets a salary of $10,000 per
year. Jennings has $10,000 on the Times;
Croly has $5,000 on the World; Hudson had
$20,000 on the Herald, and was retired on
a pension of $10,000.
    AN unimaginative individual on visiting
the Falls of Niagara was greatly perplexed
at the astonishment expressed by his com-
panions, and one of them exclaiming to him,
"Is it not a most wonderful fall?" replied,
"Wonderful! no; I see nothing wonderful
in it. Why, what's to hinder the water
from falling?"
    A SCHOOL-boy being requested to write a
composition upon the subject of "Pins,"
produced the following: "Pins are very
useful. They have saved the lives of a
great many women and children—in fact,
whole families." "How so?" asked the
puzzled teacher; and the boy replied, "Why,
by not swallowing them." This matches
the story of the other boy who defined salt
as "the stuff that makes potatoes taste bad
when you don't put on any."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE HAND THAT ROCKS THE WORLD.
	
		BY WILLIAM ROSS WALLACE.

	Blessing on the hand of Woman!
	    Angels guard its strength and grace!
	In the palace, cottage, hovel,
	    O, no matter where the place!
	Would that never storms assailed it;
	    Rainbows ever gently curled;
	For the hand that rocks the cradle
	    Is the hand that rocks the world.

	Infancy's the tender fountain;
	    Power may with Beauty flow;
	Mothers float to guide the streamlets;
	    From them souls unresting grow,
	Grow on for the good or evil,
	    Sunshine streamed or darkness hurled;
	For the hand that rocks the cradle
 	    Is the hand that rocks the world.

	Woman, how divine your mission
	    Here upon your natal sod!
	Keep, O keep the young heart open
	  Always to the breath of God!
	All true trophies of the ages
	    Are from Mother Love impearl'd;
	For the hand that rocks the cradle
	    Is the hand that rocks the world.

	Blessings on the hand of Woman!
	    Fathers, sons, and daughters cry,
	And the sacred song is mingled
	    With the worship of the sky.
	Mingled where no tempest darkens,
	    Rainbows ever more are hurled;
	For the hand that rocks the cradle
	    Is the hand that rocks the world.


CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW.

				Constantinople, Turkey, 
						April 8th, 1878. 
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	The city of Damascus has a venerable
appearance—it is very shabbily built, and
whatever taste may be internally displayed,
its private buildings exhibit none on their
exterior. We visited what had the reputa-
tion of being the best apartments in the
place—just fitted up for the reception of the
German Prince, whose arrival was hourly
expected. The reception room was a cush-
ioned siesta, open in front and facing a
square, or open court in which was a large
fountain. From this we were conducted
into a large, square room, which, strange to
[-]ay, was well lighted with glass; the front
part was floored with varigated marble, on
which stood a white marble fountain basin,
four feet in diameter, into which the water
was gently flowing—the back part of the
room was raised some six inches above the
front, carpeted, and amply supplied with
lounges, sofas, etc., all in a veritable Eastern
style, and exceedingly neat. This same
young Prince of Weimar, and suite, have
been several times on board steamers with
us—we saw them in Jaffa and Jerusalem,
where, as well as in Damascus, they were
received with a display of public honors.
	On the morning of March 19th, we left the
old city of Scriptural celebrity—the scene
of many former striking incidents; particu-
larly that of the miraculous conversion of
Paul, and having walked on the "Street
called Straight;" (which judging from the
gate leading into it, was much broader
formerly than now,)—with its one hundred
and fifty thousand inhabitants, without
shedding any tears of regret. Now, instead
instead of narrow trails, we travel on the
broad, smooth “Diligence road,” through a
narrow gorge between high rocky ridges,
overlooking narrow strips of fertility along
the beautiful Abano river, which skirts the
almost perpendicular bluffs for several miles,
and at night, camp at a village of hovel-
like dwellings, called Dimas. The next
morning, while waiting the adjustment of
tents, baggage, etc. on the pack mules, we
were much interested in seeing the numer-
ous goat-herds with their large flocks of 
[next column]
goats, starting out towards the mountains
which surrounded us on all sides; and, par-
ticularly to our amusement, numbers of
these goats issued from the doors of the
houses, to which there was but one opening.
As the goats came through the doorway,
they were intermingled with men, women
and children. Our encampment was on a
ridge of the Anti Lebanon range.
	The next day we traveled over an exten-
sive valley lying between the two ranges,
which as we approached the great range was
highly cultivated, and crowned with luxuri-
ant fields of grain; with houses representing
civilization if not refinement. Leaving this
delightful landscape scenery we ascended
a steep ridge, where we camped for the
night, in sight of lofty peaks of the cele-
brated "Mountain of Lebanon," covered
with snow; and felt the atmosphere cooled
by its frosty breath. The next morning,
as we ascended the highest elevation, we
found the cold intense—banks of snow, from
time to time, lying on the road side, much
to the annoyance of our animals—they
seemed to be making its acquaintance for
the first time.
	At several points on this Lebanon range,
the view is beautiful beyond description—
not only beautiful—it is grand and magnifi-
cent, combining a portion of sublimity, that,
while you are struck with admiration, a
feeling of awe instinctively entwines around
your imagination. I was so captivated with
the wild, bold, majestic scenery of nature,
exhibited in an interminable variety of
forms, before, behind, all around me, that
many times I stopped my horse, that I
might feast my eyes on the surrounding
beauties of nature—the almost uncultivated
“Glory of Lebanon.” Some of the mountain
slopes presented an appearance of having
been terraced long ago, and in many places
the vine is seen in rows, apparently strug-
gling against the wash of storm and the
waste of years.
	The place where, for the last time we
dismounted for lunch, I shall not readily
forget. It was on a high elevation over-
looking a cluster of ridges, which rise in
proud loftiness, over deep ravines, that,
viewed from our standpoint, produced a
sense of giddiness, from their immense
depth. Although the sides of their emi-
nences are very steep, they are terraced,
and from base to summit, highly cultivated,
and covered with many varieties of vegeta-
tion; and exhibit in a striking illustration,
the effect of the skilful power of Art, in
softening, and in transforming the rough
and wild condition of Nature. To complete
this strange, picturesque scene, the broad,
smooth road, paved with white limestone,
commences a descent directly from our lunch-
ing place, and, instead of crossing the
ravines, and running in a straight line, it
winds and zigzags around, low down at the
foot of these eminences—its whitness form-
ing a marked contrast to the green verdure
of vegetation—the luxurient foliage of
shrubbery and shade trees, and the gay
colors of the flowers, already in full bloom.
	After leaving this point, which is about
ten miles from Beyrout, the road runs in
a straight direction, and, for a long distance,
is lined on both sides with fields of mulberry.
These trees have an old appearance—
the trunks being very large, and the
branches young and small from constant
pruning. The manufacture of silk is quite
a business in Beyrout, this place having
superseded Damascus, when formerly it was
carried on extensively.
	Before our arrival in Beyrout, our Drago-
man, having special business in the city,
left us in charge of a Turk who could not
[next column]
speak English, and understood it but little;
and as we preceded our pack mules with
tents, etc., he took us to a house where we
were received with tokens of kindness—
everything was done that hospitality need
suggest—we were seated in the best room
on cushioned seats—were served to tiny
cups of coffee—glasses of lemonade, etc.—
shown into their weaving establishment
with many specimens of silk manufacture—
all this time without understanding what
prompted these expressions of friendship,
for we could not comprehend a word of each
other’s language. After some time Antonio,
our Dragoman came and relieved our
curiosity, by informing us that the people of
the house were relatives of the man who
introduced us to them. Presently our tents
were pitched and we left the hospitable roof,
and invited the lady of the house with two
or three others to accompany us, which they
did. The distance was short, but others
seeing these, joined in, and then others
joined them, and by the time our tents were
reached, we had a large procession. At
this time there was a public demonstration
in favor of the young German Prince, just
entering the city, but we concluded that
ourselves drew the most attention. The
next forenoon we were visited by multitudes;
some of the ladies urging Miss Little and
myself, we accompanied them to their
homes which we found, though simple in
style, very neat.
	In the afternoon—the 22nd of March, we
mounted horses for the last time—bade adieu
to tent life, and rode to the hotel, and on
the 25th went on board the steamer Mars,
of the Austrian Lloyd line, en route for
Constantinople.
							Eliza R. Snow.

			Munich, Bavaria, April 24, 1873.
Editor Woman's Exponent:—
	Constantinople, with its many waters
and varigated land scenery—combining
beauty and commercial advantages, sur-
passes all other locations I have ever
seen. From an outside view, as we
beheld it from the steamer's upper deck,
before, and at sunrise, one cloudless morn-
ing, when the atmosphere was clear of
smoke, this great Capital of Turkey is mag-
nificently beautiful. But, like most Eastern
cities, in consequence of narrow, crooked,
filthy streets, it will not bear an intimate
acquaintance. It contains many fine edi-
fices, of which its many minaretted mosques,
are considered its greatest ornament; and
to a Saint of God, who understands that He
accepts no church, mosque or temple, unless
built by His special direction, these are of
little or no interest, only, as a matter of
curiosity. The tall, graceful, dark-green
cyprus trees, of which there are great num-
bers, especially in the burial grounds of
Constantinople, add much to the picturesque
scenery, and are higly valued as being pro-
ductive of health, by absorbing malarious
effluvia from the atmosphere.
	I was interested, and much amused with
the novelty of the bazars—so very unlik
anything I had seen. In the first place,
you enter a narrow street or defile, with
rows of small shops with open fronts, ranged
along on each side, with articles for sale on
shelves at the back and sides, and strung up
in front—a vendor standing in the center,
ready to wait on you. After going a short
distance, other alleys or lanes lead off in
various directions—then others branch out
from them, and so on, till without a pilot,
you may be entirely lost in a forest of bazars,
and your sight bewildered with their daz-
zling displays.


﻿The exterior of the Grand Sultan’s Palace
is a rare specimen of taste, chaste beauty
and elegance.
	On the 5th of April, again on the upper
deck of the steamer Mars, I took a farewell
view of the Turkish Capital, while its hills
and dells, minarets, domes, towers and masts
faded in the distance, as well as that im-
mense structure on the coast of Asia Minor,
celebrated as the place where Florence
Nightingale bestowed her womanly and
heroic aid in behalf of the sick, wounded and
dying.
	Near the shore of the beautiful Island of
Syra, we changed steamers, and on the
morning of the 7th, landed at the Piraeus—
took carriage—rode five miles, which brought
us to the city of Athens. I am not an
enthusiastic admirer of ruins, and time-
worn relics of what is dead and obsolete,
especially when they have no possible bear-
ing on present progress, yet, in spite of my
positive predilections, my attention was
strongly riveted to some of the stupendous
remains of the ancient splendor and mag-
nificence of a city that figured so much in
the classics, and political history of Greece.
	But, as I am not a professional archoeolo-
gist, I shall leave the description to others.
While visiting the Acropolis, on the lofty
height of the plateau, a natural enormous
rock foundation of the original Athens, we
had a charming view of the present.
	Anciently, the Areophagus or “Mar’s
Hill,” was a judgment seat, where the most
important civil and ecclesiastic cases were
tried and sentences pronounced. On this
hill the Apostle Paul stood and addressed
the people. I almost fancied him occupy-
ing that strikingly peculiar position—in
close proximity to, and almost surrounded
by temples of the greatest possible brilliancy,
dedicated to, and containing vast numbers
of those venerated deities, the very gods
which he repudiated in the audience of
their worshipers; boldly telling his idola-
trous hearers that “God dwelleth not in
temples made with hands”—“we ought not
to think that the Godhead is like unto gold,
or silver, or stone, graven by art and man’s
device,” etc.
	We were invited to tea at the residence of
the American Minister, Mr. Francis and
lady, where also we met Mr. Goodenough,
American Consul General to Constantinople,
and spent a couple of hours very sociably and
agreeably. We saw George First, King of
Greece, on the sidewalk, in plain citizen
costume—were told that he is doing all in
his power to better the condition of the
people. His wife, Olga, is niece of the
Czar, Alexander II, of Russia, thus uniting
Danish and Russian element.
	We left Athens on the 12th—arrived by
train at Trieste, Austria, the 17th; at Verona,
Italy, the 19th, and on the 21st at Munich,
Bavaria.
	Of all sight-seeing and entertainments,
nothing has pleased us more, and interested
us so much as the packages of letters and
papers which we met at Constantinople and
Trieste, from Utah, informing us of “home,
sweet home.”
							Eliza R. Snow.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿Who shall succeed Salmon P. Chase,
as Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of the
United States? Bulwer makes his “Riche-
lieu” say “The mate for beauty should be
a man and not a money chest;" so the choice
for Chief Justice of the United States, should
be a jurist, not a politician; and a jurist of
the highest capability and honor, calm, con-
servative, of sound judgment, swayed ever
bya consciousness of the vast responsibilities
placed upon him.


WOMAN’S VOICE.
			Harrisville, Weber Co., Utah,
							May 11th, 1873.
Dear Editor:—
	I write feeling a lively interest in your
valuable paper, and knowing it is the privi-
lege and duty of all lovers of Zion’s cause,
to throw in their mites, to assist in bringing
to light the sacred truth, and making honor-
able mention of the kind dealings of God.
I perceive the set time to favor Zion has
come; when woman, the weaker vessel, can
do much to advance the cause of truth on
the earth, although wicked men are seeking
to take from her her natural rights, and
Satan stirreth up their hearts to do her
injustice. We, in Utah, have, and still
shall have the right to say who shall be our
leaders, lawgivers, and representatives; and,
if I may be allowed the remark, husbands
to lead us back into the presence of our
Father and our God. And now while we
have thousands of ways to make ourselves
useful in our families, and in all the great
and small duties of life; relieving the poor
by large or small donations; visiting the
sick; administering words of comfort and
consolation—we have also the great and in-
estimable privilege of worshiping God
according to the dictates of our own con-
science, believing in polygamy as the
patriarchal order of marriage, through which,
(when rightly entered into, and lived in,)
we enjoy the great blessings of the ancient
patriarchs and prophets with their families.
Oh! happy daughters of Zion. Oh! happy
Saints of God! do we realize the great and
glorious privileges we enjoy? Never was
woman blest with greater freedom. Still
we are called the “down-trodden women of
Utah!” We ignore the idea, and truthfully
attest that we have more privileges than
any other women on the face of the earth.
	Our Relief Society here is under the
guidance of worthy and competent sisters
whose whole aim seems to be to advance and
promote the cause of God on the earth.
								E. S.

	Now that there is such ample room open-
ed for our present, as well as future, profit
and usefulness, if we do not accept the
chances given us we can no more complain
for that complete advantage that the world
has so long withheld from woman. What
should be woman’s aim? Some ladies use
the plea, that the requirement of those call-
ings (which have been heretofore man’s ex-
clusive privilege) will strip them of their
delicacy, refinement, and of their feminine
attractiveness. Genuine worth need not
fear discovery. If a lady has a knack at
numbers, and would make an excellent book
keeper, where is the harm? If her abilities
fit her for a position behind the counter, let
her skill be tried; or if blessed with sufficient
natural talent to become an artist, her
endowments must not be hid under a bushel.
Girls, you who are so pale, and wan, and
pretty (!) do not scoff at this; your own
welfare and that of your posterity depends
in some measure upon woman’s cultivation
and aggrandizement. Listless purposes and
languid exertions will never exalt one.
Work with a will and a noble determination
to do the world some good. The excuse is
offered that being fitted for and filling those
mentioned places, husbands and children are
neglected by wives and mothers. The
question immediately presents itself, Does
not woman neglect her family for dress,
visiting, and amusements? But we do by
no means intend to insinuate that there are
duties belonging to motherssuperior to the
care of their children. How many are not
blessed with children? How many poor
girls without homes? How often do we
hear the phrase, a “strong minded woman,”
sneeringly uttered? Shall we devote our
admiration to weak minded women? Is
the feeble proselyte to society to be prefer-
red to the girl who is bent on nobly fashion-
ing the character God gave her, and gaining
a profession that will fill her purse as well
as her mind? Woman’s true nature is not
thoroughly alert until aroused by the wish
to do something that is independent. Do
not mistake my meaning. There is a vast
difference between a silly, conceited and
arrogant independence, and the honorable
desire to be helpful and useful. May the
ladies of our Territory improve the rich
advantages laid before them! Persever-
ance will complete success, and it has been
truthfully said, “what has already been done
may be done again.” In many ages of the
world women, with voice and hand, have
acquired distinction and honor for the noble
and brave efforts they have made. Have
not the daughters of Zion every thing to
encourage them! With an inspired leader
to assist, admonish and counsel, what
triumph of heart and brain may they not
achieve!
								Kent.


﻿We Chronicled, last issue of the Expo-
nent, the death of more than forty persons
by the falling of a bridge over the Rock
River, at Dixon, Illinois, during the celebra-
tion of the ordinance of baptism in the water
below. Now we have to record another of
these oft recurring “accidents,” which oc-
curred on the 22nd ult., during the laying
of the corner stone of a new Roman Catholic
church, in Pennsylvania. A temporary
flooring, on which a multitude had assem-
bled, gave way and precipitated them to the
ground beneath. A great many were in-
jured, but none killed so far as reported.

	The Brave Illinois Girl Who Saved
a Ralway Train from Destruction.—
As a little girl, Lizzie Bourland by name,
who resides at Woodlawn, Ill., on the line
of the St. Louis and Southern Railroad,
was passing down the track yesterday after-
noon on her way to the next station
(Roache’s,) she discovered a lot of brush
upon the track, which she concluded to
remove. Upon lifting a portion of the brush
she discovered a tie across the track. she
endeavored to remove the tie, but being
a child of only thirteen years of age, her
strength was inadequate. Just then, to her
horror she heard the train coming. With
presence of mind far exceeding her years,
she grasped her parasol and signaled the
train to stop, keeping it in violent motion
until she heard the engineer whistling down-
brakes. As the train was only about
one hundred yards distant when the signal
was given, of course the engine struck the
tie, notwithstanding the air-brakes were
put on to the fullest extent, but the speed
was so lessened by the time the obstacle
was struck that no damage was done. The
conductor of the train stopped and took up
the little girl and carried her to her desti-
nation, after thanking her for the timely
warning which, doubtless, saved a number
of lives. The train was a passenger train
bound West, and due in St. Louis at 4:32,
which point it reached in safety, through the
thoughtfulness and presence of mind of
the little girl.—“St. Louis Globe, May 8th.”


﻿Miss Edmonia Lewis, (colored,) our
famous American sculptress in Rome, has
two orders of $50,000  from British
noblemen.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


FLOWERS.

	Civility costs nothing and buys every-
thing.
	Do not meddle with business you know
nothing of. 
	Where manufactures flourish, land and
its products are most valuable.
	Every man complains of his memory,
but no man complains of his judgement.
	Keep good company and let not your
associates have cause to shun or be ashamed
of you.
	Nothing condemns more powerfully the
violence of the wicked than the moderation
of the good.
	It is with our judgements as with our
watches, none go just alike, yet each be-
lieves his own
	The first ingredient in conversation is
truth, the next good sense, the third good
humor, and the fourth wit.
	Carefully, avoid vices which most
resemble virtue; they are a thousand times
the most ensnaring of all vices
	Candor in some people may be compared
to barley-sugar drops in which the acid
preponderates over the sweetness.
	Serenity of mind is nothing worth,
unless it has been earned; a man should be
at once susceptible of passions, and able to
subdue them.
	Nature is very careful to provide against
scarcity. In the moral world it is the
same. Goodness and beauty lie thick
around us, in spite of sin, and sorrow, and
wrong of all kinds.
	Man is like a snowball. Leave him lying
in idleness against the sunny face of pros-
perity, and all the good that is in him melts
like fresh butter in the dog days; but kick
him around, and he gathers strength at
every revolution.
	The foundation of domestic happiness is
faith in the virtue of woman; the founda-
tion of political happiness is confidence in
the integrity of man; the foundation of all
happiness, temporal and eternal, is reliance
on the goodness of God.
	The angels are beautiful because they
are good," and there is no true, enduring
beauty that comes not from goodness shin-
ing through the soul's face and form, as
home-lights gleam out through the night’s
brooding shadows, to tell of all that home
means to the weary and hungry of heart.
	Though sometimes small evils, like in-
visible insects, inflict pain, and a single
hair may stop a vast machine, yet the chief
secret of comfort lies in not suffering trifles
to vex one, and in prudently cultivating an
undergrowth of small pleasures, since
very few great ones, alas! are let on long
leases.
	Too great a desire to please will often
give a person the appearance of being both
deceitful and insipid; while perfect indiffer-
ence to the opinion of others will make one
seem boorish and hateful; but an even,
straightforward, independent course, with
a generous appreciation of the good feelings
of mankind, never fails to win respect, con-
fidence and friendship.


﻿The number of persons run over in the
streets of Paris averages 1135 annually.
	The greatest cataract in the world is the
Falls of Niagara, where the water from the
great upper lakes forms a river three quar-
ters of a mile in width, and then, being
suddenly contracted, plunges over the rocks
in two columns, to the depth of one hundred
and seventy feet each!


TRUE LOVE.

	Never be ashamed of ever having loved any
one. If perchance you have hated, then blush
for it, but not for love. It does not matter
at all whether the person on whom your
affections fixed themselves reciprocated the
sentiment. Where there is no shame in
loving, in itself, the fact of having given
love without reward can bring none with it.
You have only bestowed a gift more price-
less than any jewel can be, upon one who
did not thank you.
	Since there is sorrow to one’s self in it,
it is best to struggle with the heart, and
keep it until it is asked for; but if it goes
forth, despite all effort, there is no need to
feel like a guilty thing, and Iong to hide
from your very self. Providence gave you
that great love, and I believe that somehow
it will mingle with the life it hovers over,
and shed a perfume and lend a sweetness to
it, though it has never been spoken.
	Many a woman's life has shrivelled away
under the weight of "disappointed love,"
merely becaue her shame in it was so  great.
The false sentiment that teaches her to
scorn a natural feeling has worn her beauty
away, and robbed her of all hope in the
presence of the future. I think it would be
better if even a woman dared to say, "I
loved him but he did not love me,” with
the same sweet sadness which, when years
have glided by, she can utter the words, “I
loved him, and he died.”


﻿Miss Van Lew, the postmistress at Rich-
mond, has again run the gauntlet of criticism,
and been confirmed by the Senate. There
was a strong effort on the part of the politi-
cians, who coveted so good a place for them-
selves, to prevent her appointment and
afterward to defeat her in the Senate, but
the President insisted on appointing her on
two grounds, that she gave important
information to our army during the siege of
Richmond, and that she made a good officer.
	Clara Louise Kellogg did a generous
thing at the Continental Hotel in Philadel-
phia, the other night. Having a spare hour,
she invited the hired help about the house,
who could be spared from their duties, to
assemble in the ladies’ dining-room, and
she then played and sang for them a choice
selection of her best pieces, interspersing a
number of Irish ballads and negro minstrel
melodies. The servants enjoyed it, all over,
and Miss Kellogg is sure of good attendance
in her future visits at the Continental.
	Asiatic men verily believe that chaos
would come if women were allowed to go
abroad with uncovered faces; but the ex-
periment has long been tried in Europe and
America without producing any alarming
results. It was thought in old times that
public speaking would be fatal to the useful-
ness and modesty of women, but the
Quakers long ago settled that question.
Their women have always been as free to
preach as their men, and  are pre-eminent
for their personal modesty and household
thrift.


HOUSEHOLD TREASURE.

	Every Family should have a Washing Machine;
 nd there is none better—if as good—we truthfully
and earnestly consider, having had experience in
working and taken pains in observing several kinds,
as the Telegraph Washing Machine, which is
manufactured in this city, and sold by George God-
dard; to whom all orders concerning it should be
addressed, "P. O. Box 474, Salt Lake City,"
	We take pleasure in recommending this Machine
to the ladies of Utah, and advise them to prove its
superiority by purchasing and using it.
