﻿A Conscientious Pittsburg man prom-
ised his wife, the other day, that he
would’nt drink another drop as long as he
had a hair on his head. That very night
he had his head shaved smooth, and then
got drunk, with a proud consciousness of
having faithfully kept his promise.


Woman's Exponent.
VOl. 2. SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, September 1, 1873.	 No. 7.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	Political meetings, held in different
States of the Union, indicate that the wars
of the politicians are not yet an end.
	A sad story is told of a fire, which de-
stroyed the “Widows' and Orphans' Home"
in Kansas City, Missouri, a short time since,
rendering its inmates houseless.
	Shooting is becoming about as common
as divorces were a short time ago.  What
will be the next sensational strike of the
fashionables, by way of settling accounts?
	That Queen Victoria is a “liberal” in
politics, the English Tories, it appears, have
but lately discovered.  Yet why the belie
should have been entertained that she was
ever anything else, is not made manifest.
	Maggie Elphick, the Greenwich heroine,
has been presented with an elegant gold
watch and chain, by the father of the boy
she lately saved from drowning.  So much
more, for being a swimmer and cool headed!
	Miss Lottie Moon, has been selected as a
missionary to China, from Catersville,
Georgia. Doubtless the selection was a very
appropriate one, and Miss Moon will do
great good among the "children of the
Sun."  
	The advocates of “woman suffrage” in
England, as well as in this country, still
persevere in their efforts to obtain for
women their just privileges and rights.
We hope, ere many years, to see the subject
of “Woman’s rights” generally  understood
far more correctly and fully than it is at the
present time. 
	A Senator elect, declares that members
of Congress will have families in Washing-
ton, and it is better that they have their own.
That will do, provided  the people are not
taxed for the support of those families; and
if Congressmen have become so attached
to their home circles that their hearts
cannot bear separation for a few months.
	The fair taxpayers of San Francisco
have determined on having fair play in
future. They have met, and “resolved”
that until they are represented in the legis-
lative assemblies of the nation, they will
pay no more taxes.  Why should they?
They have right to a voice in making any
law which they are expected to respect and
help to support.
	Miss Nelly de Matamarre de Treby,
a young French lady—who during the war,
plunged a knife into the breast of a Prussian
officer, who, in her father's house, ventured
to put his arm around her waist—has lately
taken the veil. Had it been the "bridal
veil” there would have been more cheeri-
ness in the thought than there is in the con-
templation of her being a nun.
	The first instance, so said, in which a
white man has been sentenced according to
law, to suffer death for killing an Indian,
has occurred in Idaho. The criminal James
Picket, about a year ago, killed an Indian
woman almost instantly with a miner’s
pick.   He was  tried, convicted and sen-
tenced to death by the civil court of Idaho.
The Attorney General refuses to recommend
that he be pardoned;
	Several newspapers are now published
[new column]
in the United states, of which, portions are
under the editorial control of ladies. This
is as it should be. But when women thus
favored, make mention of “those naughty
men,” they should never forget to make
exceptions; keeping in mind that the men
are not “all” selfish braggarts. They call
for “fair play”—they should, then, be care-
ful not to manifest a disposition to take
more than their own just rights.
	Party feeling among politicians is pre-
senting rather foreboding aspects in different
localities. The Liberals, Democrats, Repub-
licans and all kinds of office seekers in
nearly all the States are shaking fists and
making grimaces at each other. Bickering
and backbiting will continue to grow worse
and worse, apparently, until a Wasington,
or some kind of a washing machine can
be obtained, of sufficient force to cleanse
the country of some of its corruption.
	One of the most fearful railroad disasters
yet recorded, recently occurred on the
Chicago and Alton railroad, from the collid-
ing of a freight train and a passenger train.
Many souls were instanttly hurried into
eternity, and many others died subsequently
from injuries received—as recorded, not
less than one hundred in all! And this
time, the recklessness of the conductor of
the freight train, whose name is Bean, is so
strikingly apparent that officers are in search
for him; and he will likely be brought to
justice 
	Pioche has had one of the most terrifiic
storms we have lately seen recorded. In
ten minutes from the time the storm com-
menced, a dispatch states, that “a sheet of
water three feet deep was boiling and cut-
ting through the entire width and length of
Main street, sweeping everything in its
path.” And the rain continued pouering
incessantly for two hours! Some of the
sins of the Piochers ought to be washed
away, certainly, quite an amount of their
property went at any rate.
	Mayfield, Santa Clara County, Califor-
nia, has an ambitious man, Mr. William
Paul, who is likely to do good service to the
country, and also enrich himself by engag-
ing rather extensively in the silk manufac-
turing business. He has now a great
number of mulberry trees four years old,
and has lately erected a large cocoonery in
which a party of experienced silk growers
will be employed. A considerable amount
of means might be profitably invested by a
few Utah capitalists in the production of
silk, and thus furnish suitable employment
to a goodly number of females.
	The Prince of Wales has occasion to be
prouder than ever of his royal title, since
the great triumph of the chorists at the
Christal Palace—the sweetest if not the
most glorious and famous victory which has
been recorded for ages. On the 10th of
July, a grand trial of musical abilities was
made at the above named palace in London,
by English and Welsh singers, the latter
proving greatly superior to the former, and
carrying off the £l,000, which was the prize
offered to the winners.  Marked attention
and honors were afterwards bestowed upon
them by the Prince himself and his royal
consort.
[new column]
	It is scarcely realized by most readers
how the slightest omission or misapplica-
tion of a point in punctuation, a word or
letter may completely change the meaning
of a sentence. Or what great care must
necessarily be taken by those who write and
prepare copy for the press, and also by the
compositor, not to mislead their readers.
An instance occurred in one of our editorial
notes, in the last sisue of the Exponent,
in which, by the omission of a quotation
mark, we made it appear that “Grace Green-
wood” is almost a believer in the “Mormon
system of marriage,” which, we think is
not the case, though she is really a sensible
woman in most respects.
	“The Balance” says, “The Salt Lake
Exponent doesn’t tell us anything about
the divorce suit of Mrs. Brigham Young No.
17.”  By way of explanation for this seem-
ing omission, we beg to inform our Chicago
friend that our columns have been crowded
with other matters which were of greater
weight in our estimation. We believed,
at the beginning of said suit, it would all
end in “nothing,” and considered that
a journal as young as ours is, and as popular
as it is becoming, should yet continue to
treat its readers to “something.” We,
however, take pleasure in referring “The
Balance to the “Deseret Evening News,”
or Salt Lake “Daily Herald,” papers of
longer standing and greater experience
than the Exponent, for information con-
cerning the Ann Eliza Webb Young divorce
suit, if it is really wanted.  We venture to
suggest, too, that “The Balance” should
learn the truth before publishing news con-
cerning matters of importance, as the fol-
lowing statement which it has is entirely
incorrect: “Brigham claims that she has a
living husband from whom she is not
divorced—this was no objection to her
while she was content to remain an inte-
grant Mrs. Young.”


Grand Rapids, Mich., has a woman sex-
ton.


THE LATEST CASE OF MONMANIA.—A
very “fast” young gentleman, who fancied
himself a pendulum, and always went upon
"tick," went on so long that he never dis-
covered his delusion until he found a "turn-
key," by whom he was carefully wound
up in the Queen’s Bench.—“English Paper.”


A MISAPPREHENSION.—"Are you fond of
novels, Mr. Jones?”—“very,” responded the
interrogated gentleman, who wished to be
thought by the lady questioner a lover of
literature. “Have you,” continued the in-
quisitive lady, “ever read "Ten Thousand
a-Year?"—“No, madame; I never read so
many novels in all my life.”


YANKEE "ENTICEMENT.—An American
sailor lately gave evidence before a court of
law at Boston in case of assault. He stated
that “ Jack” (the traverser) had powerfully
enticed Sam (the complainant). Jack's
counsel asked him to explain what he meant
by the word entice. “ Why, sir, I mean
this—Jack took a big handspike, and drove
it plump in Sam’s back. That’s what I calls
enticing.”


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


[For the Exponent.﻿
		A WARNING.

TO THE OVER-WORKING ONES.
				Stop now!
Listen to the working brain—
'Tis trying hard your ear to gain,
List n to its dizzy whirl— 
It seems the steam is in a boil.
And needs a fresh supply of oil,
As if it did most surely try,
Off in a tangent for to fly, 
And get away from toil and sweat,
And leave “unfinish’d rooms to let !”

					Stop now, 
Attend ! and bend your ear to know
That heart’s quick palpitating throe,
That every minute takes a rest— 
Sure this should be a warning test—
And then succeeds the weak’ning limbs,
The warning lamp, that nothing trims,
Repose alone can feed the fire,
That lacking rest, will soon expire,
Be wise “in time”— and if your health,
To you is truly "mines" of wealth
			Stop now!

So I listen'd—and I found
Enough of heart and brain still sound,
		To redeem
		The whole machine, 
If I put “the Brake” upon 
All the work through which I’d gone;
So right there, and then, I dropt
All my labor, and I stopt, 
And shook myself from every chain
And rambled on the mountain plain, 
A free-born spirit once again,
Without an ache—without a rain,
	A world of wealth    
	In luscious health,
	Because I heard
	The spirit word,
	(And did obey
	Without delay)
			Stop now.
				Hannah T. King.
G  S. L. City.


﻿R. S. REPORTS. 
	Spring City, August 5th 1873. We held
our annual meeting, June 6th, which should
have been held May 4th. but on account of
sickness which prevailed at that time,
meeting was dispensed with for fear of
spreading the fever, which seems to have
subsided now. President Olesen was pre-
sent; he spoke very encouragingly to the
sisters, said they had gone for beyond his
expectations, and thought they had done
exceedingly well under their circumstances.
He gave some very good advice.
	We have been taking your paper the
past year and wish to continue taking it, it
is a very good little paper and we wish it
good success.
We remain as ever yours respectfully
				Mrs. P. Benson.
		-------------------
	Meeting at Mendon, August 7th, 1873.
The meeting was openend in the usual
manner, by singing and prayer. After
which, Sister Maughan, of Logan, gave us
a very interesting discourse upon the duties
of the sisters, some of the chief points being
for them to take proper care of their chil-
dren, to train them up in the way they
should walk; to be careful and close their
doors before retiring to rest at night for they
knew not how soon some person, or persons,
who were not honest might make an
attempt to appropriate another’s property to
their own interest, or perhaps do worse; for
the sisters not to labor so hard, so that they
might enjoy more fully the spirit of their
calling. Her remarks were few and to the
point. Our President,Sister Elizabeth Willie,
then made a few very appropriate remarks
upon the principles of the Gospel; endors-
ing the remarks of Sister maughan. She
was followed by sisters Meria Baker and
Sophia Anderson (Counselors) who spoke
upon the necessity of our living our religion
and being obedient to the Priesthood.
[new column]
Sisters Hancock. Graham, Huges, Walker,
Sonne, Baker and Richards, each made
remarks, speaking on various subjects of
interest which were beneficial to those
present. The meeting was then dismissed
by singing and prayer. We wish the EX-
PONENT, success and its Editor prosperity,
	Yours 
		ELIZABETH WILLIE, President.
		E. J. BAKER, Clerk.
			——
	BRIGHAM CITY, August 10th, 1873.
DEAR EDITOR—I appreciate your excellent
paper and know that many of my sisters
receive it it as a blessing, wishing it great
success and considering it an instrument of
great worth, in disseminating light, know-
ledge and truth, with every principle
calculated to enlighten, exalt, and prepare
us for a broader sphere of action; I wish it
a more extensive circulation. The mem-
bers of our Relief Society generally feel
well, and are ready to cast in their mites to
assist in the work which lies before us; we
raised upwards of one hundred and twenty
dollars this spring, for the special purpose
of assisting in gathering the poor, which,
with what we had previously gathered for
that purpose, amounted to over two hun-
dred dollars, this amount we sent to assist
in emigrating Saints this year, while we
are trying to do the best we can with our
means for the benefit of our poor at home
and our Society generally.
		HARIET A. SNOW, President
				--------
	August 11th, 1873. The Tokerville Relief
Societywas organized June 11th, 1868.
Wo have not made as much progress as
some, but as a Society, are trying to do the
best we can. The sisters are united in their
efforts to do good. We have helped the
poor at home and abroad, and donated what
we could for the building of the Temple, we
also have shares in the Washington Cotton
Factory and the Co-operative store at this
place—besides some means in the Treasury.
	Yours respectfully,
		SARAH M. WILLIS, President.
		FANNY SPILSBURY,
		ANN HIGBEE		Counselor
		ANNIE JACKSON, Secretary,
		ANN HIGBEE, Treasurer.
	DEAR EDITOR:—I send you a brief report
of a meeting held August 6th, 1873, at the
residence , of Brother E. R. Young, Sugar
House Ward, President Mrs. Sarah Gibson
presiding. After singing and prayer the
financial report for the past year was read,
and satisfactorily received. Much good
instruction was given and a good spirit
prevailed, many sisters bearing their testi-
monies, and expressing their willingness to
do all in their power for the upbuilding
of the Kingdom of God on the earth.
	With kind wishes for the success of the
EXPONENT, I am.yours affectionately,
		SARAH E. WHEELER, Secretary.
	Miss M. A. Jenkins, Secretary of the
Young Lady’s Rretrenchment Association
of the 4th Ward, favors us with minutes of
their Twenty-Second meeting, held in 4th
Ward Relief Society Room, July 15th, 1873.
	After the opening ceremonies President
Miss Mary E. Bailey spoke as follows: It is
a very difficult task for me to arise and
speak to you; still, I wish to do all that
is required of me; and as I am placed in the
position of President, I know it to be my duty
to set an example by speaking. If we come
to meeting with light hearts and determin-
ation to perform every duty, the Lord will
bless and help us. I would like all to attend
[new column]
the meetings in the 14th Ward as often as
possible. By listening to the instructions
given there, we are better able to speak to,
and instruct others. We miss our late
President much; feel that there is one
missing from the circle and that we have
lost a friend and a leader: for she did lead 
us with kindness: she encouraged and
helped us, and set an example we all should 
strive to imitate, by learning her lessons well, 
and attending our meetings punctually. We
will ask our older sisters to come in occa-
sionally, and talk to us and cheer us up. I
would like to hear each one speak a few
words. I want you to help me all you can, 
and let us be interested and do all the good
that lays within our power to do.
	Counselor Miss Ceelia Condie said: I am
pleased to meet with you again. I often
think of the instructions our late President
gave us, which we should try to remember
and profit by. Let us not indulge in
“novel” reading but rather read our own
Church books. I have neglected to read
good works as I should have done, but with
the help of the Lord I intemd to reform.
	Counselor Mrs. Mahalia Jenkins said:  If
we neglect our duty we are liable to grow
lukewarm and indifferent. I feel to sustain
our President and do all in my power to
assist her. I am satisfied that she will do
her best. Let us all try to speak; the Lord
has said of His servants, that if they stand
up and try to bear testimony, He will put
words into their mouths; and I think He
will do the same for us.
	Miss LoviniaHaigh made a short address.
	Miss Carrie Savill said she was pleased
with the meeting and would like very much
to become a member of the Society. Miss
Lizzie Edwards, Miss Hannah Jenkins; Miss
Sarah Jenkins, Miss Lucy Smith and Secre-
tary Mary A. Jenkins, each made a few 
remarks after Which, some new resolutions
wore read.
	President Miss Mary E. Bailey stated that
about the last request their President made
of them before her death, was to keep their
meetings, and what was said in them, per- 
fectly sacred; and Miss Bailey desired all
to remember it.
	Appointments were made for the next
meeting, and meeting was adjourned for
two weeks.
	Singing, “Lord dismiss us," etc.
	Benediction by Counselor,  Mrs. Mahalia
Jenkins.
				——
	Tho following is from Cedar City, Iron 
County, Aug. 12th, 1873.
	On Thursday, the 26th of June we held our
annual meeting in the Social Hall. Meeting 
opened, by singing,“Come let us anew” etc.;
Prayer by Counselor Annabella Haight;
singing, “Arise O glorious Zion,' etc; Min-
utes of previous meeting read and accepted.
The reading by the Secretary of the yearly
financial report showed that there was a
little means still in the Treasury and that
the articles donated had been economically
disbursed. Remarks by President Mrs.
Rachel Whittaker in which she advised the
sisters to donate liberally of their means to
help gather the poor; mothers to set a good
example before their children, to teach
them to pray and send them to Sunday
School, and recommended family and secret
prayer. Several of the sisters expressed
their willingness to help to gather the poor.
Benediction by Mrs. Ann Elizabeth Webster.
				ELLEN LUNT, Secretary.
	Accompanying the above report was an
address composed by one of the sisters and
read at the supper table of a Surprise party
given to their President, Mrs. Rachel
Whittaker, in commemoration of he[-] 5th


Birthday, in which the warmest feelings of
love and confidence towards that lady, as a
worthy pattern, were expressed.

	Report from Centreville, August 15th,
1873. Our Relief Society was organized
June 21st 1869, and reorganized Feb. 12th
1876 with Mrs. Margaret H. Randall Presi-
dent, Mrs. Rebecca Porter and Mrs.
Emeline Smith Counselors, Mrs. Mariette
C. Adams Secretary and Miss Sarah L. Smith
Treasurer. As the place is small we don't
gather in as much as they do in larger
places, but the people have responded liber-
ally to the calls that have been made. The
President is ever willing and ready to
engage in any good work, and the sisters
that attend the meetings are united. We
feel that we have done some good in help-
ing the poor at home and abroad and hope
to do more in the years to come. A small
house with a piece of ground were donated
to us by Mrs. Margaret Cherry. By the
liberality of the brethren the house has been
enlarged and we have a comfortable place
in which to meet. The EXPONENT is a
welcome little messenger to us.
				M.C. ADAMS, Secretary.

	In Ogden on the 14th ult., a Relief Society
meeting was held, at which were present
Miss Eliza R. Snow, Mrs. M. I. Horne, Mrs.
Margaret P. Young and Mrs. L. Greene
Richards, from Salt Lake City; and many
officers and members of societies belonging
to adjacent settlements, forming, in connec-
tion with the Ogden Society, a large congre-
gation. The meeting was held in the 
Tabernacle. President Franklin D. Rich-
ards and other Elders on the stand, also
President Mrs. J. S. Richards with her
Counselors and other officers, and the visi-
tors. The congregation was addressed by
Miss E. R. Snow, Mrs. M. P. Young, Mrs.
M. I. Horne, Elders P. Richard, F. D.
Richards and Bishop Thomas, the general
spirit of the teachings being for improve-
ment in all the ways of life. Miss Snow's
address will probably be given in full in
another issue of the EXPONENT.
	
	Minutes of the Sixty-Fourth meeting of
the Senior and Junior Co-operative Re-
trenchment Association, held in the Four-
teenth Ward Assembly Rooms, August 16,
1873.
	Meeting opened with singing, "Joy to
the world;" Prayer by Counselor Mrs. E.
Howard: Singing, "An Angel from on
high."
	President Mrs. M. I. Horne remarked
that in looking over the congregation, she
thought how few were present in compari-
son with the number of ladies there were
in the city; considerable talking had been
done to the girls of late, but she thought
the mothers in Zion needed talking to quite
as much as their daughters; if mothers
would set their daughters worthy examples,
they would have all influence with them.
It seemed that the teachings were lost on
many, on account of their indifference, yet
it was the duty of each one who had received
knowledge of the truth, to use all the in-
fluence possible for the benefit of her
neighbor. The speaker referred to a meet-
ing she had lately attended at Ogden, in 
company with Sister Snow and others from
the city, and mentioned the number of young
ladies, one hundred and five, who belonged
to the Society of Sisters in Ogden. She
then gave the meeting into the hands of
the sisters, many of whom bore faithful
and interesting testimonies to the truth of
the Gospel.
	Miss Eliza R. Snow said she believed
[new column]
it right and incumbent upon the sisters to
read good books and get all the useful
knowledge they could in relation to the
cause of diseases and how to treat them; in
as much as the Lord had endowed them
with faculties, it was for them to cultivate
and use them; but their chief dependence
and reliance, they should ever keep in mind
was in the power and spirit of God, and
that He alone was able to strengthen and 
preserve the works of His hands. When
young people could be made to understand
the spirit of the Gospel, they had no more
need to be warned against the influence of
the spirit of the world, it was as uncongenial
to their feelings as to those of older people
who realized its follies. If young ladies
were wanted who could be depended upon,
the Retrenchment Associations were the
places in which to look for them. Speaking
of the importance of the present day, Miss
Snow said, "Let every woman who  has
light within her, warn her neighbor, for
this is a day of warning.”
	The congregation sang “We thank thee
O! God, for a prophet.” Other addresses
were made and meeting adjourned for two
weeks.
	Singing, “Praise God from whom all
blessings flow." Benediction by Counselor
Mrs: Z. D. Young.
		MISS M. A. JENKINS, Secretary.

	FOUNTAIN GREEN, SANPETE Co., Utah,
					August 14th, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	DEAR SISTER:—It is somewhat a late
date to report to you.
	Our Society was organized on the 11th of
June, 1868, and has been in a prosperous
condition ever since. We have donated
liberally to the poor in our ward and have
made them as comfortable as our means
would allow. We have also donated to the
Temple at St. George. We own $50 in the
capital stock of Fountain Green Co-operative
Mercantile Institution, and have, in the
Treasurers hands about the same amount.
	We have no poor in our Ward that are in
need. The word "Female" was dropped
from our Society at a meeting held in Decem-
ber 1872 by a unanimous vote of the sisters.
We hold our meetings regularly every
month, and a good spirit has always pre-
vailed, and we hope ever will.
	The EXPONENT is a welcome visitor and
we wish it much prosperity. Our President
is upheld by her Counselors, Teachers and
sisters in general. The health of the people
in these parts was never better than at the
present time.
	Yours in the good work,
		POLLY ANN JOHNSON, President.
		ELIZABETH F. Johnson, Secretary.

	A report is given from the 3rd Ward in this
city, of a meeting held August 20th, at which
Mrs. W. Margarett Steel and Mrs. Emma
Wagstaff from the 1st Ward, and Mrs.Sarah
wheeler from the Sugar House Ward were
present. Mrs. Wheeler spoke of the beauty
and comfort she found in her religion, it
was everything to her and her great desire
was to live it and have her family live
according to its perfect principles.
	Mrs. Steel wished the circulation and
influence of the EXPONENT to be increased,
as she felt it to be a means of doing much
good among the sisters.  She hoped the
3rd Ward all attended the General retrench-
ment meetings held in the 14th Ward, all
should go there and hear the good instruc-
tion which were given. President Mrs.
Weiler said she always very much enjoyed
going to the sisters’ meeting in the 14th
Ward, wanted to see the EXPONENT well
[new column]
patronized and sustained. The members of
the 3rd Ward Relief Society were trying to
be faithful and diligent in the performance
of their duties. She hoped they would all
speak their minds freely, that their visitors
might not go away disappointed. Mrs.
Wagstaff said she felt at home among her
sisters of the Third Ward; spoke of the
calamities which were sweeping the earth,
and of the necessity of instructing the young
in the ways of life and of their religion;
they should be taught great care in regard
to the company they kept. Addresses
followed by sisters Fanny Smith, M. Blythe,
E. A. Gleason, M. A. Angel, Z. G. Eardley,
E. P. Weiler, E. Haynes and Elizabeth
Gregory. One of the sisters said her time
was so much taken up with worldly cares that
she had no time to read or write, and hardly
knew when Sunday came; yet she desired
to live her religion and hoped she did not
do wrong. Secretary Mrs. Eardly asked if
it was right for the time to be wholy spent
in the cares of those things which pertain
to this life alone. She often felt grieved
that she had so little time to spend in read-
ing and studying to gain intelligence and
lay up treasures of which no one could rob
her. The young ladies, meetings and Sab-
bath Schools were referred to and all were
advised to encourage and help them. Meet-
ing adjourned for two weeks; dismissed
with singing, and prayer by Counselor Mrs.
Fanny Smith.
	MRS. ELIZABETH WEILER, President.
	MRS. ZURVIAH G. EARDLEY Secretary.

	From the Secretary of the Relief Society
in Paradise, Cache County, we have
received the following: “Our Society was
organized four years ago; the President,
Sister Jennie James, is energetic in her
endeavors to do good. At out last meet-
ing she spoke upon the necessity of the
sisters seeking to improve their minds and
gain understanding; and also of their useing
the abilities with which the Lord has blessed
them, in assisting to carry on His work.
She urged them all to take and read the
EXPONENT, and to write also, and help to
bear testimony to the world, that the
women of the Latter-day Saints are “truly
free,” that they speak and act and write with
freedom, and are not restrained by any
other power than that of their own con-
science and their own feelings and desires.
	And now I wish to give a few of my views,
humbly and in sincerity; for I believe that
the Lord now requires of His daughters
that they, as well as the Elders, speak in
defense of the Truth which He has revealed,
through His wonderous mercies and match-
less grace. I have been a member of the
Church twenty-five years; I entered it for
the sake of the truth, which I love. I have
lived in plural marriage, ten years, and this
order I know for myself was revealed by
God as a portion of a great plan of redemp-
tion for a fallen world. I do think that
every woman who has received the light and
made covenants in the house of the Lord,
should seek, with all the wisdom wherewith
she is endowed, to aid in spreading the glad
tidings of peace and salvation abroad, and
in allaying and transubstantiating the preju-
dice which exists against the works of God
in the Latter-days. I, for one, shall deem
it a great privilege if I, in my simplicity,
can say or write any little word that may
comfort or encourage, or in any way bless a
sister, or a friend, for my whole desire is
to do good in some way.
	You, dear Editor, and a few others have
braved the storm—God bless you! And
may we, your sisters, have courage to step
	CONTINUED TO PAGE EIGHT.


(CONTINUED FROM PAGE THREE.)
forward and add our little offerings, remem-
bering that mountains are made by the 
unity of many mites, and that altogether, 
we may accomplish great and glorious
works; even such as the enemies of right-
eousness, the vain and ignorant will not
dare to scoff at.
	My thoughts have been awakened, while
my soul sickened with the news of a beloved
friend having strayed into darkness and
wandered away from the Truth! And I felt
that the sisters should be on their guard and
watch themselves closely, that they say
nothing and allow no thoughts to remain
with them which may grieve away the
good spirit. But they should converse and
write freely about those things which per-
tain to eternal life and happiness much more
than most of them do; and they should
strive to reason and reflect understandingly
of their own condition and the condition of
the world; and pray always, that they be not
led into temptation and drawn away from
the light. I hope that all my sisters may be
led to realize the great responsibilities which
rest upon them, and strive diligently to live
in a manner, becoming Saints of God, that
His Spirit may guide them into all truth
and righteousness.
	This is my first attempt at writing for
the press, but, however, it may not prove
my last, for I feel it is well for us to try to
build each other up, and thus by united
efforts to sustain the Priesthood.” 
					SINAH BISHOP.

			PLEASANT GROVE CITY,
						August 21, 1873.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
   Knowing the desire and interest you have
for the welfare of the Relief Societies, I
take pleasure in sending you a few lines
from this place.
  At our legular monthly meeting, held on
the 9th inst., as usual the members of the
Society were “generally” present and a
lively interest was manifested in the good
work in which we are engaged. Our
Bishop met with us, and by request ad-
dressed the meeting at some length, en-
couraging the sisters in their efforts to do
good in relieving the wants of the needy,
etc. Although their contributions might
appear to be small they should not be dis-
couraged, large mountains were composed
of small particles, the widow’s mite was an
illustration that it was not the great amount
contributed, but the motive which prompted
the action, that would be regarded, and
according to this, all would be rewarded.
He also gave some excellent counsel in
relation to training children; said that
mothers have, as a rule, more influence
with children in their early years than any
other person.
  Several other persons spoke and bore testi-
mony to the truth of the Latter-day work,
and all seemed willing to do to the extent
of their ability to advance the interests of
the Society.
Financially our Society is in a healthy
condition; in addition to administering to
the wants of the poor here, we have assisted,
this season, one brother to emigrate from
Denmark, whose family came here last year.
  To-day we have a general working meet-
ing of the Society, which is well attended.
Knitting, Patch-work, etc is going on at a
lively rate, giving our large hall the ap-
pearance of a hive of working bees.
  Our President, with several of the sisters
is at Provo to-day, attending a meeting held
by our General President, Miss E. R. Snow.
Wishing the success of every good cause,
[new column]
including your valuable journal, I remain,
very respectfully your sister in the covenant
of peace, 
	MISS PAULINA E. BROWN, Secretary.

   SALT LAKE CITY, SEPT. 1, 1873.


﻿SPLINTERS.
Several fires have been recently reported
in England......Carlton College, Michigan,
has received a donation of $100,000 from
Mrs. Susan Willis, of Charleston....In the Da-
nish West Indies, "reconciling" courts have 
been established, where opposing parties must 
appear and seek to reconcile their difficul-
ties before they are permitted to commence
a suit at law..... Tea merchants in England,
are being arrested, and paying fines for
selling adulterated teas.... The Sandwich
Islands social system is begining to look
rather dark to a writer in Hawaii, who
says the nation is given over to an early and
dishonorable grave.... Late advices charge
McLellan, the postal agent between Wash-
ington and New York with only $50,000
stolen and also say he has not been arrested
nor is likely to be......Indians and engines,
or Indian raids and railroad quarrels have
greatly assisted in keeping up the sensa-
tional interests of daily newspa-
pers for some months past, but seem to be
subsiding somewhat now....The Duke of
Brunswick willed all his land and personal
estate to the city of Geneva.....The Balti-
more "American" celebrated its one hun-
dredth anniversary last month.....The
"Sunday Morning News" is the title of a
new paper just commenced in Stockson,
California......A veteran of 1812 was lately
tripped up by a little pig in Georgia, the
injuries received by the fall causing his
death.....A school teacher in Vermont,
punishes bad boys by making one turn a
grindstone while another bears on it with a 
stick....Prussian troops are vacating France.
......The Shah is said to be so dainty about
his diet that nothing but certain kinds of
fish will do him......A sorrespondent de-
scribes French painting as it appears in the
art galleries of the Vienna Exposition,
as the most artistic of any......Love and 
politics are stated to be the chief causes of
insanity in Spain, where madness is fre-
quent, some of the female occupants of the
asylumhaving been brought there through
the bad effects of novel reading......Men
and women are said to be quite upon an
equality in Colorado, not only in their free-
dom of opinion but in associations and
vocations as well......Emily Faithful gives
occasional receptions at her residence in
Hyde Park, London...A miner in Pioche had a
rock fall upon him recently which weighed
about one thousand pounds, and twenty-
eight hours afterwards was alive and in
full possession of his senses, though the
spinal column was dislocated and the lower 
limbs paralyzed....A town on Kalmur Sound,
Sweden, called Christianople, has been
utterly destroyed by fire....The Massachu-
setts' Republican State Convention will be
held on the 17th inst., at Worcester....The
ship "Oregon," of Quebec, was lately wreck-
ed at Portland, Richie, in the Straits of
Belle Isle, with a valuable cargo.......Ken-
tucky black bees refuse to make honey.....
Sing Sing prisoners have found a means of
escape from the roof......Carpet worms are a 
new pest in New York, but the papers say
they are not so pety as the carpet bag-
gers......A removal of the Tombs is talked
of......Hartford is said to have more assur-
ance than any other city in the country.......
A number of Iowa men are going to "eat"
for the championship of the State, at Atlanta.
......Hack drivers in Milwaukee, have to pay
five dollars if they fail to light their lamps.
......San Jose papers publish appointments
for Senator Maclay's political speeches under
the head of "Amusements.".....In some
counties in California, the Democrats are
called Modocs, and in other, adjoining
[new column]
counties, the Independents are the Modocs.
......The editor of the Tular "Times" is
likely to get spanked by the editor of the
Mariposa "Gazette," at least the latter
threatens it, though they are about one
hundred miles apart.....The pleasantness
of the situation of Martinez, the county seat
of Contra Costa, is said to be almost unknown
to many residents of San Francisco, while
in natural advantages, the former is stated
to surpass all the towns on the eastern shore
of Suisun Bay, where it is situated....A new
kind of sea monster was lately caught in the
bay, near San Francisco, which has the
head of a shark and the body of a porpoise,
measures nine feet ten inches in length
and weighs five hundred pounds......Wit-
nesses in the Stokes' case are still confined
in prison, nor for any crime, but because
they happen to know something concerning
the shooting of Fisk......A San Francisco
editor considers the question of national
currency exceeds all others, and believes
the want of paper money to be the secret of
the depressed state of business throughout 
the country......Three women have been
arrested in Brooklyn, charged with the
murder of a man, of whom one of the
women is the widow....Hay seed in the
hair and blue drilling overalls, it is said,
will be the prevailing styles for Western
politicans this fall......Favorable prospects
for harvests in France are reported this
year......Lotta begins new scintillations in
Philadelphia this month.....The stock of lace
of the  Princess Josephine, cousin to the
ex-Queen of Spain, was lately seized by a
Paris milliner as security for an unpaid bill
of $1,059......The question is asked, when
Tweed is to be tried for his crimes, but the
answer is not given.


ANOTHER STEP.

	Believing, that if properly and intelligently
conducted, many benefits will certainly accrue
from the consummation of the given proposition
—benefits to woman and consequently to man—
and sincerely hopeing that wisdom and pru-
dence may direct every effort of those who may
engage in this worthy attempt to accomplish
great good, we cheerfully append the following
notice, of a proposed “Woman’s Congress,”
which explains itself:
“The necessity of fellowship and concerted
action among women interested in the ad-
vancement of the race and more especially of
their own sex, is so apparent, that we do not
hesitate to assert that by far the larger portion
of our efforts in that direction are fruitless, be-
cause they are solitary and isolated.
Thousands of noble and beneficent women,
scattered all over our country, are to-day think-
ing, writing and speaking the truths which all
women need, and many are waiting to hear,
and which would, at once, be a renovating
o[-]rce in the land, if the believers could, in a


congregated body unite upon practical methods
for their incorporation into government, busi-
ness and social life.
  Therefore in consideration of the present
demand for unity of method among women in-
terested in like objects, Sorosis, whose purpose
is “to render women helpful to each other and
'useful to society," has determined to take the
preliminary steps for bringing the representa-
tive women of the country together in a
Woman’s Congress, that unitedly we may take
into consideration the more important ques-
tions that affect our Woman's life.
  We send this little messenger to all women
who by voice or pen, or practical work, have
conquered an honorable place in any of the
leading reforms of the day, and we cordially
invite them to signify their approbation and
sympathy with our effort by sending us their
names to affix to the formal Call for a “Wo-
man’s Congress,” to be held in New York, in
October, 1873.
  This Messenger will also be sent to such
Women’s Associations as have for their object
the improvement of women in any direction.
  This Call will be issued as soon as we receive
sufficient number of worthy names to insure
the success of the Congress, and we shall not
issue it until we receive that encouragement.
  It is not the intention of Sorosis to take the
lead in the Congress, after it shall be assem-
bled; it will select its officers and govern its[-]lf.
We, meanwhile, constitute ourselves a local
Committee for the preliminary business. As
we, as an associate body, send out this little
sheet, we append to it the names of the officers
of the society, but we shall not affix to the
Call the names of any persons who have not
formally notified us of their willingness or
desire to have their names appear.
Friend! If after you have carefully read our
Messenger, you are willing to assist in calling
the good women of our country together for a
noble object, please send your name and post-
office address to our President or either of our
Secretaries.
MRS. CHARLOTTE B. WILBOUR, President,
			151 East Fifty-first Street, N. Y.
MRS. JENNIE C. GROLY, Ex-Prsident,
MRS. MARY F. DAVIS, Vice-President,
MISS KATE HILLARD,  		"
REV. CELIA BURLEIGH, 		"
PROF. MARIA MITCHELL, 	"
MISS ALICE C. FLETCHER, Rec. Sec.,
		25 Stuyvesant Street, N. Y.
MISS FANNY HOWELL, Cor. Sec.,
		470 Vanderbilt Avenue, Brooklyn.
MISS ROMELIA L. CLAPP,
		Chairman of Executive Committee.
	Some of the questions to be considered by a
Woman’s Congress will be the following:
Higher Education for Woman; Woman’s
Work in Literature; The relation of Woman
to her Dress; Woman, the Promoter of Peace;
Prison Reform, Woman’s work; The Inviolate
Homestead; Equal Division of the Salary be-
tween Husband and Wife; Woman’s Influence
in promoting Temperance; Co-operative indus-
tries among Women; Woman’s place in
Government Systems of Philanthrophy; Law
as it affects Woman; Enlightened Motherhood;
Woman in Surgery and Medicine.”


HOME MANUFACTURE.

	Where is the individual, the family, com-
munity or nation who, being possessed of true
independence is not justly proud of it. And
in what does independence consist, so much as
in the home production of all articles necessary
for life's comforts and conveniences?
	No family that depends on buying the shoes
and stockings, hats, coats, jackets, aprons skirts
and so on, even to suspenders and garters, ready
made—for all its members from father down
to baby—ever knows anything of the real satis-
faction of being, in a measure, independent.
	A little girl never dons a pair of boughten
stockings with the same pleasure, or feels that
they are so perfectly comfortable, as the ones
she has knitted herself. While even the latter
are more satisfactory, if made of yarn spun at
home, from wool which her own dainty fingers
have assisted in preparing for the machine.
And if the recollection of having taken an
occasional run to look after the dear, old sheep
or pretty playful lambs from which the snowy


fleeces were clipped, happens to occur to her
mind some frosty winter's morning, these soft,
warm stockings appear to her to be "thrice
blessed." No far fetched gaiters ever seem
half so nice to her as do the little neat, white
"buck-skin" shoes, fastened with delicate pink
bows, made by the skillful hands of her own
patient, practical mother. This is personal
experience.
	None are so happily situated as those who
are able to manufacture for themselves, instead
of paying out money in order to hire others to
manufacture for them. This statement is no
less true of a nation or a kingdom, than of a
family or an individual.
	The community that chops its wood with
imported axes, churns its butter in imported
churns, clothes itself with materials brought
from other countries or other parts of a country,
imports its school books and its school teachers
or sends its children abroad to be educated; and
manages all its affairs in a similar manner,
can never thrive and become wealthy like the
one which develops its own resources, and is
able to export instead of being obliged to im-
port the common requisites which belong to
civilized life.
	Co-operation is the one great principle, and
the only one, upon which this saving system of
home manufacture can be profitably introduced
into a community. If tradesmen and trades-
women, engaged in all the various departments
of useful industry, could de induced to patron-
ize and sustain each other, as much as possible
in producing, and using home productions, a
beautiful order would thus be established, in
which home manufacture would produce home
independence.
	But so long as each one demands money of
every one, for his or her work, in order that all
may trade where they will, so long as laborers
refuse to sustain each other in their home in-
dustries, there will be little independence
realized, among the masses of the people.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


HOME AFFAIRS.

	We understand that a new daily paper has
been started at Beaver, entitled "The Beaver
Eenterprise."
	There has been considerable threatening of
heavy storms in this valley of late, but as yet
no results of a serious nature have occurred.
	The report of a ladies' meeting, held at
Provo during a late visit of President Miss E.
R. Snow to that city, has reached us, but too
late for insertion in this issue.
	Mrs. Belinda  M. Pratt, of Fillmore has
recently taken the superintendency of the
Relief Co-operative Store in that city, which
she writes us has fair prospects of doing a good
business.
	It is very gratifying to find that a number
of ladies are becoming interested in writing
for the Exponent. We hope to see their en-
deavors increased, and that others will be
encouraged to engage in like undertakings.
	Some of the Relief Society reports in our
present issue are particularly interesting. There
are a few hints in the one from Paradise,
which might be considered with profit by any
woman connected with the good cause of the
Latter-day work.
	Clear cool nights and mornings have char-
acterized the state of the atmosphere in this
valley for several days past. In some portions
of Cache valley, we learn that slight touches of
frost have been apparent during the last days
of the past month.
	A Party of Editorial Excursionists, from
different parts of the Union, lately visited this
city, and as considerable pains was taken by
the Board of Directors of the Deseret Agricul-
tural and Manufacturing Society, and others.
to make their short stay an agreeable one, we
hope it proved such, and that we may hear
some pleasant reports from it.
	Interesting accounts are given of the late
Cache Valley Conference held at Logan City,
by President Young and party. Large and
attentive congregations are reported to have
assembled, as usual on such occasions, from all
parts of the valley.
	This evening is the one for the Sunday
School Convention, being the first Monday in
the month. Teachers should bear it in mind, 
and be present, that they may keep up with
the growing interests of the Sabbath Schools
in this city, and acquaint themselves with the
progressive steps of the Sabbath School Union.
	Brother John Miller, who has delivered
the Exponent to a portion of the subscribers
in this city since its commecement is desirous
of withdrawing from the services thus rendered,
and wishes to notify any lady who would like
her paper delivered for one year, beginning
with the next issue, that he will receive pay
and be responsible for its safe delivery. He
does not propose to receive any further appli-
cations after the 15th, inst.
	Bishops, Post masters, Presidents of Re-
lief Societies and other prominent citizens in
different parts of the Territory, have been
speaking good words and accomplishing good
works for the Woman's Exponent. We ten-
der our grateful acknowledgments for these
efforts from the journal's many and kind
friends, trust they may be continued with as
much earnestness and sincerity in the future
and even greater success; and that the worthy
example may be followed by many others
	President Young, with a party of Elders
from this and neighboring towns, is now on a
tour through the northern settlements of the
Saints, visiting and holding meetings. Com-
munications from members of the company
state that he is in good health, and enjoying
his labors as usual. Their meetings were ap-
pointed for to-day, to be held at Bennington,
Bear Lake Valley; the company expect to re-
turn to their homes on Saturday, the 6th inst.
	Hon. George Q. Cannon, for some years the
editor of the "Deseret News," has retired from
the position on account of a great press of
other duties, and Elder David O. Calder as-
sumes that responsible situation. Both gen-
tlemen are well known in the community as
men of great intelligence and integrity, well
worthy the confidence and good wishes of the
people. We trust the "News," under the man-
agement of Elder Calder, may continue to in-
crease in usefulness, meeting in the direction
of its calling, the wants of the growing popula-
tion and the desires of its new editor.
	A school for ladies, who design studying
and becoming conversant with the science of 
obstetrics, will be opened in this city, in a few
days, by Mary H. Barker, M. D., whose card
appears in this issue of the Exponent. The
lady, we understand, is fully competent to give
a thorough classical course of that which she
undertakes to teach, and is prepared to open a
class as soon as books which have been sent for
arrive from the East. The class will be held
at her rooms, where she will be found by any
lady wishing to make arrangements with her
concerning the school, during her office hours,
which are designated in her card, also her place
of office.
	The Ogden "Junction" tells us about the
Bear River Valley Railroad, which it shows
will soon prove, in mor ways than one, a very
great benefit to the Territory, if the work on it
continues without hinderance, as projected.
It will run from Hilliard on the Union Pacific
Railroad, Southeasterly for twenty miles to
the head of Mill Creek, a tributary of the river.
And it is believed that through this medium,
the best of lumber can be furnished to the
markets cheaper than it can be imported, as
the line will run in easy distance to not less
than 25,000 acres of excellent timber. Other
important advantages are also stated as bear-
ing connection to this new railroad enterprise.


[For the Exponent.
			REASONING.

	I have been reasoning within my own
mind upon the subject of reading fiction, and
concluded that it is entirely wrong and
detrimental to our best interests. It often
seems a fine way to spend our leisure
moments, and entertain those around us.
But it, is not truth, therefore it must be
falsehood; and it appears that we love, and
if we don't make lies we tell them, and the
Scriptures say that whosoever loveth or
maketh a lie shall have their portion in the
lake that burneth with fire and brimstone.
I can now see that this practice is both fool-
ish and wicked, and am fully resolved that
however highly colored and fascinating the
fiction that comes in my way I will shun it
as I would the coils of a serpent which
would extinguish life. For novel reading,
if persisted in, will uproot our love of truth,
fill our minds with trash, leaving no room
for useful knowledge; useing up money and
our precious time, grieving the Holy Spirit,
and leading us down to death. How different
the opposite picture, if our thoughts are
filled with truth, theology, scientific litera-
ture and all the knowledge of God and
our relationship and duties to Him, that we
are His offspring; that He has given us
organizations in His own image, both phys-
ically and mentally, the right use of which
will exalt us in His presence to thrones,
principalities and powers—imparting all
true knowledge as circumstances may re-
quire.
	If we in our duty to Him do good to all;
if we feed the hungry, clothe the naked,
visit and administer to the wants of the
sick and afflicted, then we shall have our
reward. For the Savior will say as is re-
corded in His word "Come ye blessed of my
Father, inherit the Kingdom prepared for
you before the foundation of the world."
For I was hungry and ye fed me, thirsty
and ye gave me drink, naked and ye clothed
me, sick and in prison and ye came unto
me. "In as much as ye have done it unto
one of the least of these my brethren, ye
have done it unto me." Now, the Relief
Societies have done this to all the hungry
and destitute of this Territory that they
could search out, and they will have their
reward. And still there is room. We must
"work while the day lasts."
	The rules given by an ancient philosopher
to his pupils, I think are worthy of applica-
tion to ourselves.
	
	"Nor let soft slumber close your eyes,
	Before you've recollected twice
	Thy train of actions through the day,
	'Where have my feet chose, out their way.
	What have I learned wherer'e I've been,
	from all I've heard from all I've seen—
	What duties have I left undone,
	Or into what new follies run?'
	These self-enquiries are the road
	That leads to virtue and to God."
					Excellette.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


TRIBUTE OF RESPECT.

   TO THE MEMORY OF BROTHER JOSEPH W.
				 YOUNG

	He lives in the hearts of the people he loved,
		In the hearts of the orphan, and widow, yes; 
	In the hearts of the aged, the lonely, and poor--
		He lived but to comfort and bless.

	Oh Friend! in the days when friends were so few
		The day of thy rest, and glory has come;
	How “Blessed the dead who die in the Lo d,”
		And their labors do follow them home.

	Though the mantle of sorrow falls heavy and dark,
		And thy loved ones must mourn for their Head,
	God pity them! grant them that Spirit of thine, 
		That will sweeten the tears which they shed.

	For the good of the Kingdom his life has been given,
		'Twill be said, “though his body lies low,
	  In the battle of Truth against error he fell
	With his face toward heaven, his f et to the foe !"
	
	Oh true tender heart! farewell for awhile, 
		We all the dark Valley must tread.
	And the humble and true shall meet thee again
		Through the merits of Jesus our Head.
					Agness S, Armstrong.
Ephraim June 15th 1873


[For the Exponent.
		ASSERTION IS NOT PROOF.

	How tiresome it is to have a person con-
tinually thrusting his opinions into your
face, with all the assurance of an oracle,
without ever condescending to give a
reason. You cannot venture your modest
opinion on any subject whatever, without
coming in contact with one of these unsup-
ported, stonewall “surmises” which pertly
assume to be facts. You think the air feels
damp as if a shower was coming; but Mr.
Positive, with great assumption of superi-
ority informs you that “the atmosphere to-
day is dry as a bone.” You think the
use of tobacco injurious to the health–––
“Madam, it is the very best tonic known.”
	You think the free school system a great
blessing.—“Yes, to parents and teachers,
but perfect robbery of those who are
neither.”
	No amount of persuasion could elicit from
him an argument in support of his mighty
opinion, but he expects you to accept un-
questioningly his most absurd dogma as a
fact incontrovertible. And you meet these
plagues in every walk in life.
	Some gravely tell you on no account
to pet and fondle your children unless you
wish to ruin them; but not being able
to see where the harm is, you venture to
inquire how and why that would ruin your
darlings, “Oh I see you don’t believe it,
but you will when it may be too late,” and
that is all the information you can obtain.
	A physician tells you a certain kind of
poultice is good for that swelling on your arm,
but if you ask “How is it good, Doctor?”
you receive a cold stare and the reply “To
make it well;” and it requires real courage
to persist, “Now, Doctor, I must have a fair
answer; if that poultice is “good’ to scatter
this inflamation through my system again,
I do not want it; I want something to
bring it to a center and throw off this im-
purity while there is a chance.”
	You take an interest in the study of
Phrenology and are curtly told it is a perfect
waste of time. Being only in search of
instruction, you mildly inquire “why” and
are wonderfully enlightened by the lucid
“Because there’s not a word of truth or sound
sense in it.”
	You sympathize with the Woman’s
Rights movement and honestly believe that
the mothers of men and women have as
deep an interest in their present and future
welfare as the fathers, and, therefore, would
be if as well educated, quite as capable of
properly shaping their destinies. But you
learn with wonder and awe that “Woman
never was and never will be capable of un-
derstanding political economy nor of ac-
quiring a man’s education.” Having the
testimony of all sacred and profane history
that women have been chief rulers, high
priests, counselors, commanders and war-
riors, you suppose, in your innocence, that
you are to receive some new light on the
subject, but instead of that, your informant,
looks the picture of injured innocence, that
you ever imagine that statement needed
confirmation. Surely you do not doubt his
veracity?
	Now what “I” want to know is just this:
Am I obliged to swallow all these nauseous
doses of wisdom and call them, “good” for
me, or may I speak my mind, and say “If
you can give me a reason sufficient to con-
vince my judgement, then I will accept your
views, if not, I shall consider myself at
liberty to esteem my own opinion as highly
as yours?”
	If in order to be womanly and keep my
“sphere,” I must do the former, then let
spheres take care of themselves for I’ve no
use for them. I do not feel like “giving
proof" of woman’s inferiority by any such
compliant course. Every person, within
whose soul the least spark of reason exists,
has a right to cultivate that reason and give
it satisfaction before adopting any principle
or opinion; and because my head may be
weaker than yours and my judgment less
reliable, is no reason I should not cultivate
and improve them. And when you give
me your views on any given subject and
withhold the support of these views, you
defraud me of an opportunity to cultivate
my judgment and arrive at a just conclusion
for myself.
	You have no more right to force your
unsupported belief on my acceptance, than
Mahomet to spread his religion at the edge
of the sword. If I cannot see as you do
when you are right, that is my misfortune
and I deserve your pity. If you wish to
benefit me by your opinion, show me kindly
the foundation on which your confidence
rests, and then if I cannot see its beauty and
durability, let me alone until my own
experience and observation confirm your
teachings.
	Even the child, the little one of tender
years and immature faculties, has a right
to expect a reason for anything under the
sun which it is taught; and when one is
not given, the child has a perfect right
to reject the teaching.
	“In my opinion,” if this principle were
more generally acted on, a great amount of
existing bigotry and intolerance would be
swept away, and vastly more brotherly
love and charity possess the hearts of
humanity. 
						L. L. D.
BEAVER, Aug. 8th, 1873.


Phraseology.—A gentleman who had
just completed a tour through the wilds of
the West rode up to a house in Illinois,
around which were more improvements
than were common to the country. On in-
quiring of the owner if he would sell the
premises, he replied he would. “What do
you ask?” was the second interrogatory.
“I ax a good deal.”—“How much is that?”
“A right smart chance of money.” —“What
is a right smart chance of money ?” “A heap
more than I gin for it.”—“Well, sir, what
is your price, now, per acre?”—“I’ll allow
I don’t want to sell it.”


THE GREAT CONFLAGRATION.

	The following vivid description of the
late Oregon fire is from the "New North
West" of August 8th. 
	Portland lay asleep. All over the city
the Peace Angel hovered, and the dreams
of thousands as they slumbered in [--]ied
security, were unbroken by premonitions of
danger. But the incendiary did not sleep.
With his fiendish appetite whetted beyond
its ordinary wont, he prowled among the
sun-dried buildings wherein yet greater
combustibles were stored, and at the silent
hour of four, the hour when sleep is sound-
est and danger and suspicion of danger
is supposed to be at rest, he applied a torch
that laid a city low. Well did he lay his
foul purpose. Firemen, watchmen and
policemen were alike lost in the sweet lux-
ury of a morning knap. The prolonged
whistles of steamers at the docks for many
minutes seemed to only woo the dream god.
But at last a solitary fire bell rang out its
sharp alarm. Portland awoke—awoke and 
flocked to open windows, with drowsy eyes
and disheveled hair, with blanched cheek
and fluttering heart and gazed out upon
the great mountains of smoke, surmounted
by slender spires of lurid flame. Another,
and yet another fire bell rang. Men hurried
on their clothes and darted down the ere-
while deserted streets. Women, scarcely
taking time to clothe themselves, followed
at a little distance. Thousands gathered at
the scene of disaster and gazed and strug-
gled, till lot after lot and block after block
fell before the devouring element, driving
back, with the fierce tongues of fire that
possessed them, or the great billows of
smoke that they sent forth, the frantic
owners who fought for longer mastery in
vain.
	Women, with little children clinging to
their clothes, struggled along under all sorts
of burdens. Men, divested of all thought
of self, fought the arch fiend face to face,
saving property wherever they could.
Brothels emptied the low, vile, horrid life
with which they teem into the open streets,
and filled the air with curses. Ministers
stood upon the steps of costly churches
within a stone's throw of these awful dens,
and lifting up their hands and voices, be-
sought the God of elements to stay His
hand. Men, more practical, enshrouded
buildings in wet blankets and prayed with
hose and buckets, and, we trust, silently as
well. Other men, by dozens, became bru-
tally intoxicated, helplessly leaving their
wives and children to bear the burdens of
the day. Our noble firemen  came from Salem,
Oregon City and Vancouver, and chiefly by
the aid of these reinforcements was the con-
flagration headed off before it could destroy
the St. Charles Hotel, the only brick build-
ing that stood between the conflagration
and the almost entire remaining front. The
loss is computed at $1,185,325; insurance
$258,000. It is a greater calamity to the
Northwest than the Chicago fire was to the
central portion of the union, for it is a far
greater loss in proportion to the age of the
country, its wealth and population. But 
our people are again at work. Nothing
daunted, they will not say die. Already
preparations are being made to rebuild
many business houses. Relief committees
will see that the destitute do not suffer, and
though the years will pass away before Portland
recovers from her shock, she will rise from
her ashes, better built than before.


Lucca has been divorced.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


RIGHT KIND OF A FARM HOME.

	[The following pleasant sketch of what a
farmer's home should be is from “Harper’s
Magazine” for February. Farmers as they
build homes will do well to pattern after the
picture first described:] Early in October,
Lance and Laura were married, and moved
into their new home. It was far from being
a fashionable or imposing residence, unless
we except Laura’s one extravagance—the
little bow-window; but it had an eminently
cozy, homelike air. The moment you step-
ped inside, you received a comfortable,
cheerful impression, as if here were a place
where people were in the habit of enjoying
themselves.
	Entering a little square hall--on one side
was the dining-room; on the other, the par-
lor; back of the parlor, the bed-room; the
furnace imparting a summer temperature,
the doors of their adjoining rooms all stood
open, giving good air, and a deal of roomi-
ness for so small a house. The parlor paper
was a green and gilt flower on a light drab
ground; the carpet an ingrain, small checks,
green the predominant color. Through the
bow-window the sun shone brightly in over
Laura's plants, making a summer within,
even if it were winter outside. Each side
of the bow-window, on little brackets, were
Parian busts—Eve and Psyche—wedding
presents, looked out from English ivy that
twined around them, and then met over the
hanging basket in the middle of the win-
dow.  On the walls hung two or three good
engravings and photographs; over them
clusters of bright autumn leaves. A set of
hanging book-shelves, bearing the united
library of Lance and Laura, presented an
odd combination of poetry and works on
Agriculture and "The Horse." Then there
was a lounge which "was" a lounge—not a
rack contrived to exasperate the human
frame to the utmost by its’ knobbiness—an
easy-chair a camp chair, a shaker rocking-
chair, one or two cane-seated chairs, a centre-
table with the big lamp, books, papers, and
Laura's work-basket.
	This was the family sitting-room. Look-
ing in of an evening, you would have seen
Lance on one side of the table in the big
easy-chair, reading his paper, or chatting
with Laura, sitting opposite in her shaker
rocker with  sewing. One great advan-
tage in marrying a farmer is, that you have
him at home with you evenings, provided
you make yourself tolerably aggreable to
him. Laura, even if she were married,
still thought it worth while to fashionably
arrange her hair, wear the bright bow, the
dainty collar, the little et cetras that really
add so much to woman's attractions. Lance
had too much respect for Laura and himself
too to sit down for the evening in his old
frock, tumbled hair, overalls tucked into
coarse boots, savoring strongly of the barn-
yard. He brushed his hair, donned an old
coat and slippers, and so, with a little trouble,
gaining vastly in comfort and his wife's
affections.
	From their windows the light of a happy
home streamed, cheerfully out, a benediction
to the passer-by. People were fond of drop-
ping in there for an evening, it was "so
pleasant," they said. Many a farmer's boy
and girl, after an evening at Lance's, went
home thinking farming wasn't so bad, after
all, and they wouldn't be in such a hurry to
grow old enough to leave for the city, if it
could be as pleasant at home. For fashion in
Knipsic Farms had generally ordained an
entirely different order of things from that
prevailing at Lance's. The parlor of every
respectable farmer must contain a very hard
and slippery hair-cloth, sofa, six chairs, and
a huge rocking-chair possessing the same
qualities in even a greater degree; other
furniture to correspond, arranged at stiff
angles around the walls. This sacred ap-
partment, as well as the whole part of the
house, was kept cold, dark, shut up, and
suggestive to the bold invader who dared
penetrate its dreary shades only of funer-
als. The family lived mostly in the kitchen,
sustained, probably, by the proud conscious-
ness of possessing a best parlor and hair-cloth
furniture. Passing by at night, you would
think the house uninhabited, did not a ray
of light from way back in the L reassure
you. Did company come unexpectedly, so
great a parade was made of building fires,
opening rooms, getting out the best things
that the unfortunate guest felt he should
never dare come again. So Lance and
Laura were unconsciously doing missionary
work that a farmer's home need not neces-
sarily be destitute of any desirable comfort
or refinement.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

ADVICE TO LADIES UPON COSTUME

	In no way has civilized man played more
fantastic tricks, and sacrificed his reason
more entirely to folly, than in the matter of
dress. The clumsy and inconvenient gar-
ments of the savage, are attributed to his
ignorance of domestic arts; but what can be
said in excuse for civilized man, when he
wears shoes that project half a yard beyond
his feet, or exchanges his own locks for
a periwig filled with powder and pomatum;
when the graceful motion of a lady’s head is
sacrificed to the stiff movements necessary
in balancing a tower of linen and wire,
half a yard high, with draperies that flow
from the top of it to the floor; when the
wavy lines of a female form are disguised
under a stff circle of whalebone, which im-
prisons the body from the hips upward, and
a buckram cage so surrounds the lower limbs,
that she can with difficulty walk or sit.
Some false standard of beauty, invented
perhaps to conceal some deformity, is set
up, and then the very bones and mucles of
the perfect body must be made to conform
to it. When this is carried so far as it is in
the case of small feet in China, its absurdity
strikes us at once; but we may find, nearer
home, instances of a standard as false, and
consequences even more fatal to health and
happiness than the little feet of the Chinese.
	The history of national costume in the
civilized countries of Europe shows, that for
many centuries the progress of art and
manufactures only led to greater extrava-
gance in dress, and more preposterous
fashions. One enormity was only displaced
to make way for the opposite extreme, as
in the case of the peaked-toed shoes already
mentioned; these were followed by shoes of
only the length of the foot, but as broad as
they were long. At one time men’s coats
were so short that they resembled boy’s
jackets of the present day, and soon after
they were so long and full that they looked
like female attire. Women's sleeves were
sometimes made so long that they were tied
in knots, to prevent the wearer from tread-
ing on them, and nine yards of cloth was a
moderate quantity for each sleeve; and then
they were made as tight as skin, and
reached no father than the elbow. It would
seem that for centuries the whole ingenuity
of a nation was taxed to invent monstrous
forms of clothing, as well as inconvenient
and useless appendages, and that comfort
and ease were the things most of all to be
avoided in dress.
	After the general fashion of hoops had
passed away, and when they were gazed
after, on gala days only, as a raree-show, it
was difficult to imagine rational beings
reconciled to them, and considering them
an indispensible part of female attire, or to
believe that they were once so universal in
London, that maid-servants were seen
washing down the door-steps in hoop--petti-
coats. Yet such was actually the case before
they were left off by the higher classes.
	This reflection upon past fashions should
make us look with jealous eyes on those
which prevail in our day, in order to dis-
cover whether we are not in the practice of
something which will appear equally absurd
to future generations. The French nation
has long been the arbiter of fashion for most
civilized countries and the political revolu-
tion of the last century was accompanied by
a revolution in dress almost as signal; for it
banished wigs and buckles, powder and
pomatum, stiff stays and full petticoats,
long waists, and high-heeled shoes. One
extreme led to another, and the ladies who
had been encased in whalebone, bukrum.
and abundance of quilted petticoats, stepped
forth as Grecian goddesses, without any
corsets, any petticoats, any fulness to their
garments, or any heels to their shoes.
White muslin dresses of the scantiest
dimensions, drawn closely round the figure,
with the shortest possible waists, and not a
fold or a plait that could form any drapery,
were the order of the day in France and
quickly spread into England. Only look
at the portraits in the historical pictures of
that period—such for instance, as the coro-
nation of Napoleon—and you will be so
shocked at the tight sleeves, short waists, and
narrow skirts of the women, that it will be
difficult to believe that such a style of dress
ever looked well.
	The influence of fashion is so strong in
corrupting the eye, and perverting the taste,
that it has led some persons to doubt the
existance of any true standard of beauty as
applicable to costume; but as long as some
forms of dress, when out of fashion, look
better to us than others, we may fairly con-
clude that there are some immutable princi-
ples of taste connected with the subject,
and that those articles which we admire,
after they have ceased to be the reigning
mode, conform in some degree to true taste.
Such for instance, is the simple cap called
after Mary Queen of Scots. When the pre-
vailing fashions are most opposed to the flat
shape of the Mary-Queen-of-Scots cap, it still
appears to us beautiful; and when adopted
by a modern fine lady, as her costume in a
portrait, or her dress at a masquerade ball, it
is pronounced to be highly becoming. Now,
this is not the case with the monstrous
constructions of gauze, ribbon, and wire,
that was called caps fifty years ago; nor with
any of those head-dresses, which outrage
common sense, and set all proportion at de-
fiance. As soon as the enormous horse-hair
cushions, over which the locks were combed
and plastered with powder and pomatum,
went out of fashion, that style of head-dress
was condemned as hideous.
	Now, if there are principles of true taste
involved in the mysteries of a lady’s toilet,
is not the study of them worthy of a refined
and intellectual being; and would not her
time and thoughts be better spent in con-
forming her style of dress to them, than in
eagerly following every change of fashion
dictated by the love of novelty, apart from
real beauty?—“English Paper.”

	Paint.—To get rid of the smell of oil
paint, plunge a handful of hay into a pail
full of water, and let it stand in the room
newly painted.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


HELP YOURSELF.

	Fight your own battles. Hoe your own
row. Ask no favors of any one, and you will
succeed five thousand times better than one
who is always beseching some one’s influ-
ence or patronage. No one will ever help as
you can help yourself, because no one can be
so heartily interested in your affairs. The
first step will not be such a long one, per-
haps; but carving your own way up the
mountain, you will make one lead to another,
and stand firm in that while you chop still
another out.
	Men who have made fortunes are not
those who have five thousand dollars given
them to start with, but boys who have
started fair with a well-earned dollar or
two. Men who have acquired fame have
never been thrust into popularity by puffs
begged or paid for; or given in friendly
spirit. They have outstretched their own
hands and have touched the public heart.
	Men who win love do their own wooing,
and I never knew a man fail so signally as
one who induced his affectionate grand-
mama to speak a good word for him.
Whether you work for fame, for love,
for money, or for anything else, work with
your hands and heart and brain. Say “I
will,” and some day you will conquer.
Never let any man have to say, “I have
dragged you up.” Too many friends some-
times hurt a man more than none at all.––
“Grace Greenwood."


DIVERSIONS.

	The Shortest Day.––The day before
that on which you have a bill becoming due,
which you are unable to meet.
	An American editor cautions his readers
against kissing short girls, because this habit
has made him round shouldered.
	“Jem, you’ve been drinking.”—“ No, I
haven’t; I’ve been looking at another man
drinking, and it was too much for me.”
	Slow Work— Making an auger hole with
a gimlet, eating soup with a fork, and run-
ning to fires on crutches.
	What are you writing such a big hand
for, Pat?” “Why, you see, my grand-
mother’s dafe, and I’m writing a loud letter
to her!
	To kiss a rosy-cheeked girl and find your
mouth filled with Venetian red, and the
girl grown suddenly pale, is truly awful.
	Refinement. — A servant-maid, in a
small town in Herefordshire, last week left
her situation in consequence of her being
incapable of "reconciling her feelings to the
very bad grammar spoken by her mistress.”
—Country Paper.
	Anecdote of Northcote.—One day Sir
W. Knighton called upon the veteran and
asked him, "What do you know of the
Prince Regent?" “Nothing," said North-
cote; "what should I?" “Why, he knows
you very well," said Knighton. “Who
says so?” "Himself” "Poo!” said
Northcote; “it is only his brag."
	Somewhere in the West, a sable knight
of the lather and brush was performing the
operation of shaving a hoosier with a dull
razor. “Stop!" said the hoosier, “that
won’t do.” "What’s the matta, boss?”
“That razor pulls.” “ Well, no matta for
dat, sah. If de handle of de razor don’t
break, de beard’s bound to cum off!”—
Pittsburg Mercury.
