WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
	  SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, SEPTEMBER 15, 1873	    No. 8.


EDITORIAL NOTES.	

	"Dolly Vardens" is the title of
political party that figured conspicuously
the recent California elections.
The Pope has been ill; once such a rumor
might have startled all Europe; now it
forms a brief paragraph for the newspapers.
Such is the change fine brings.
The British government on the 9th
Inst., through its agents Sir E. Thornton
and Mr. Archibald, paid the fifteen and
a half million dollars, awarded by the Gen-
eva arbitration tribunal to the United States
government.
Recently a man in Virginia City, 
Nevada, was fined $25 dollars for striking a
squaw in the face with an orange. We are
glad to see that even an Indian woman can
sometimes have justice done her. 
Trowsers with one leg is the latest
name given to the civilized feminine style
of dress. At least this is the appelation
used by the fair ladies of Persia to convey
their idea of a European lady's dresses.
Garrit Smith presented Susan B. An-
thony with some money towards paying the
fine imposed on her for practically testing
her right to a Constitutional vote; but in
our opinion spoiled the value of his gift by
depreciating fidelity to the Constitution.
Late Telegrams indicate that Peru is
determined to advance in education. Free
schools are established there, and the Peru-
vian Congres has passed a free school law
requiring citizens to school their children
or suffer the penalty of a heavy fine.
The Prince of Wales keeps at Marlbor-
ough House a mark-book in which is noted
the progress of his children in their studies
and their good deportment. One excellent 
plan that was practiced by his father, Prince
Albert, and one which other parents  might
try with good results. 
Severe injuries to a farmer and his
wife, the death of two of their children, a
horse killed and a wagon smashed to frag-
ments, were the results of the farmer's
trying to urge his team across the track just
before a coming train, near Galion, O.,
lately. The danger signal was given but
the engineer could not prevent the fearful
accident. A sad, and we trust a useful
warning to persons who, in their impatience
might attempt to run a similar risk.
A movement is reported [--] foot in St.
Paul, Minnesota, to lessen the social evil by
publishing in the city newspapers the names
of all who are known to visit houses of ill
fame. If the proposed plan be carried out
honestly it would be, we think, much more
likely to effect good results than the infa-
mous plan of licensing such places. And it
would be more likely to strike at the root
of the evil by holding up to public contempt
those who patronize and encourage prosti-
tution. 
Reports of seven fires in as many dif-
ferent places, not including the burning of
seven cars on an Eastern railroad, the loss
of twenty-five lives at Havana, where
parents threw their children from the bal-
conies to save them from the flames; and
the destruction of property loss by one fire
[new column]
not given, valued in the aggregate at
$800,212,00 was noted while glancing over
a morning newspaper; no particular issue
being selected. Is not the above significant
of the signs to appear in the last days?
The following may not be new to some
but we deem it too good to pass over:
M'me Bres has received the degree of
Doctor of Medicine, in France. Her certifi-
cate runs thus: M'me Bres, a married
woman and mother of two children, does
not study medicine as a pastime or by rea-
son of scientific vanity. She fulfils all the
duties of her profession. During both
sieges of Paris she never left the bedside o
our wounded soldiers.”   When woman,
to practical benevolence, courage and gentle-
ness, also adds the requisite knowledge
and skill, acquired by close application, she
may perform, deed that would grace an
angel of mercy."
One of our exchanges, “The Pioneer,”
actively San  Francisco weekly devoted to
the interests of woman has had an editorial
change; Mrs. E.P. Stevens after a five years
successful management retires from the edi-
torship, and Mrs. C. C. Calhoun assumes the
native duties of journalism with the follow-
ing line of conduct marked out, “viz; The
unflinching championship of Woman's Suf-
frage as it is understood before the world in
the right of woman to approach—and partic-
ipation in the privilege vouchsafed in the
imperishable Constitution of the United
States of North America—Only this and
nothing more, and this too, strictly and
“only within the law of the land we live
[--]
Persons should be very cautious about
trying to get on railway cars when in
motion, for even those constantly employed
about a train and who are supposed to be
familiar with its every movement are not
always safe. Several serious accidents in
this Territory have occurred through at-
tempting to get on or off the cars when in
motion, of which we are reminded when
reading a telegraphic account of a terrible
accident that occurred on the Lake Shore &
M. S. R. R. A young man employed as
brakesman jumped on a car which was
moving towards him, but his foot slipping,
was caught in the machinery and notwith-
standing his struggles to save himself he
was fearfully mangled, and death soon came
to relieve him of his sufferings. 
When people are about to build, though 
their means may be limited, they should
consider the probabilities and wants of in- 
door life; and while aiming to adapt their
dwellings as far as they can, to secure
ventillation, roominess and convenience,they
should also remember that the bed-room is
not or should not be the least important part
of the house; that a small back room will not
do as well as any place, while the front, best
and, perchance, the largest room is reserved
for occasional use when entertaining visit-
ors. When people take a proper amount of
sleep they spend on an average, nearly a
third of their lives in bed, and when sick
or perhaps suffering from an accident which
confines them indoors and most likely to
their chambers. they need all the invigorat-
ing influences that nature supplies in the
[new column]
form of fresh air, sunshine and pleasant
prospects. For these reasons, a southern or
eastern aspect should be preferred; the ceil-
ing should be high, and the mode of
ventilation as convenient and ample as is
possble; while the windows should be high
and free enough from drapery to admit
plenty of light and the carpets and furniture
not so good that the sunshine will be ex-
cluded for fear of spoiling them. Light is
a far greater curative agent than many
suppose. It is asserted that Sir James
Wylie, late physician to the Emperor of
Russia, having attentively studied the effect
of light as a curative agent, in the Hospital
of St. Petersburg, discovered  that the
number of patients who were cured in rooms
properly lighted was four times those con-
fined in dark rooms.  This led to a complete
reform in lighting the hospitals of Russia,
and with the most beneficial results." Who
is there that having suffered with protracted
illness does not know the pleasure with
which the weary sufferer longs for, and
hails the coming dawn. Then let the sun-
shine in and with it more health and happi-
ness.


MORE THAN twenty years ago, the "New
York Gazette" published the following
argument, used by a canal stockholder, in
opposition to railways, which might now
be considered to have had a touch of the
"prophetic," in the main, though humorous
in style: "He saw what would be the
effect of it; that it would set the whole
world a gadding. Twenty miles an hour,
sir! Why, you will not be able to keep an
apprentice boy at his work; every Saturday
evening he must take a trip to Ohio to
spend the Sabbath with his sweetheart.
Grave plodding citizens will be flying about
like comets. All local attachments must
be at an end. It will encourage flightiness
of intellect. Veracious people will turn
into the most miserable liars; all their
conceptions will be exaggerated by their
magnificent notions of distances. 'Only a
hundred miles off! Tut, nonsense, I'll step
across, madam. and bring your fan!'—'Pray,
sir, will you dine at my little box at Alleg-
hanny?'—'Why, indeed, I don't know; I
shall be in town until twelve. Well I shall
be there, but you must let me off in time
for the theatre.' And then, sir, there will
be barrels of pork, and cargoes of flour and
chaldrons of coals, and even lead and
whisky, and such other like things, that
have been used to sober traveling, whisking
away like a set of sky rockets. It will upset
all the gravity of the nation. If two gentle-
men have an affair of honor, they have only
to steal off to the Rocky Mountains, and
there no jurisdiction can touch them. And
then, sir, think of flying for debt. A set
of baliffs mounted on bomb-shells would
not overtake an absconded debtor, only
give him a fair start. Upon the whole, sir,
it is a pestilential, topsy-turvy, harum-
scarum whirligig. Give me the old, solemn
straight-forward, regular Dutch canal—three
miles an hour for expresses, and two for jog
and trot journeys—with a yoke of oxen for a
heavy load. I go for beasts of burden; it is
more primitive and scriptural, and suits a
moral and religious people better. None of
your hop-skip-and-jump whims now for me."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


THE WORLD OF LOVE.

It is sweet as we journey through life.
	So darkened by sorrow and sin,
So full of unbrotherly strife,
	So far from the goal we would win—

To know that a bright world of love
	Surrounds us in every land;
To know that the angels above
	Rejoice to be ever at hand

	To "lift up the lap of the dark
	And speak clearer" of that which they know;
To kindle the heavenly spark
	Which brighter and purer shall glow:

To comfort, to counsel, to warn,
	To lighten the burden of care;
To point to a happier morn,
	To banish the gloom of despair:

To give bread to the famishing soul.
	And freedom to those that are bound;
And still as the ages unroll
	Spread "more light" to the nations around:

To speak of our Father in Heaven,
	To speak of our brothers on earth,
Of the penitent sinner forgiven,
	Of death as the spirit's new birth

In a world more divinely fair
	Than foot of man ever has trod;
No sorrow, nor trouble, nor care.
	In that glorious city of God.

O, 'tis sweet to hold converse with those
	Who have gone to the world of the blest,
Where the tree of life evermore grows,
	And the soul that is weary may rest!
							Selected,


R.S. REPORTS.
	Minutes of the 14th and 16th meetings,
held by the young ladies of the 10th Ward
have been handed in, from the first of which
we select for the present issue, the following
items: Reading from the "Juvenile In-
structor," History of the Church, by Miss
Lizzie Edmunds. Miss Nettie Poll read a
poem from the Exponent, entitled 'Don't
Let Mother do it!" An original essay was
read by Miss Martha M. H. Paul. Presi-
dent Mrs. M. J. Pierce remarked that she
was pleased with the reading exercises,
especially 'Don't let mother do it!' 'Girls,'
she said, 'take heed to what is contained in
those few lines, and don't let mother work
so hard; help her all you can, and strive to
be her greatest comfort. You may not
know what trouble she has passed through
and how much she needs a comforter. And
who can be a greater comfort to a mother,
than a good, affectionate daughter? I have
heard girls say to their mothers, 'I don't
want to do this, it will soil my hands.' How
wrong and foolish! Mother has soiled her
hands many times for us all, and we can
never repay her for what she has done for
us. Instead of going out so often as some
of us do, let us stay at home and help
mother, and we will be blest.' Mrs. Ann
D. McAllister warned the young girls who
had to go out at service, against going to
fashionable hotels to work. It was no dis-
grace to work for a living but girls should
be cautious where and by whom they were
employed in these days. Mrs. A. M.
Pierce spoke of the the great necessity there
was for prayer and watchfulness. Miss A.
Graham felt that great good would result
from the little meetings which were held
by the young ladies; they were thus prepar-
ing themselves for future usefulness. Miss
Lizzie Edmunds knew that it was the Spirit
of God that brought them together with 
such happy hearts, and hoped they would
cultivate that spirit and that it might con-
tinue with them. Miss Charlottie Hile-
stead believed that, if they were united in
their efforts they would continue to improve
[new column]
and that the Lord would sanction and bless
their efforts. Mrs. H. Tout and Mrs. A.
Wilcox made appropriate remarks.
	Miss M. A. Jenkins forwarded a report of
the 23rd meeting of the Young Ladies'
Association of the 4th Ward. The meeting
was opened with singing and prayer;
catechism was recited by the members
present, and the meeting was addressed by
President Miss M. E. Bailey, Counselors
Mrs. Mahalia Jenkins and Mrs. M. A. Care-
less, Miss M. Jones and Miss L. Hague.
Original and selected articles were read and
meeting adjourned for two weeks.
		Provo City, August 21st, 1873.
Editor Woman's Exponent:—
	Yesterday, according to appointment,
Sister Eliza R. Snow, on a visit to the
sisters of this city addressed a large and 
attentive audience of the members of the
Relief Societies at the Meeting House.
Sister Holden, of the 4th Ward, called the
meeting to order, after singing by the
choir prayer was offered by Elder J.P.R.
Johnson. The choir sang "Oh my Father
thou that dwellest." Sister M. T. Smoot
then introduced the speaker to the audience,
who, after expressing her pleasure at meet-
ing so many of her sisters, and her thank-
fulness to God for his protecting care during
her Eastern tour, gave an interesting account
of her travels. In her remarks, the speaker
referred to the principle of plural marriage,
urging mothers to teach it to their daughters;
alluded to the instructions of the Prophet
Joseph Smith, that the Relief Societies
were empowered to create any officers that
were deemed necessary for the well being
of their organizations. Therefore it was
moved and seconded that Sister Margaret
T. Smoot be sustained as President of the
four Presidents of Provo City Relief Socie-
ties, which was unanimous by the vote of
sisters present.
	At the close of Sister Snow's remarks,
Sister Elizabeth Howard, of Salt Lake Co.,
read the following

			ACROSTIO
	Addressed to our beloved Sister
			Eliza R. Snow
On her first visit to Provo City after returning from
			Jerusalem.

	Each faithful Saint, without a doubt,
	Love's courtesies would fain extend,
	In fact, although we may not shout,	Zion would welcome Zion's friend.
	As such no sister that we know,
	Ranks higher than dear Sister Snow.
	Sincerely then, we wish her joy,
	Nor, only wish but would employ.
	Our means, or ought, that joy imparts,
	Within our pow'r with all our hearts.
						Henry Maiben
			On behalf of the ladies of Provo City.
After which the choir sang "Sweet bye
and bye" with chorus, which was most
beautifully and effectively rendered.
	Benediction by Elder J. P. R. Johnson.
	Minutes of the 65th meeting of the Senior
and Junior Co-operative Retrenchment As-
sociation, held August 30th, at the 14th
Ward Assembly Rooms, Salt Lake City.
Meeting opened by singing, "Arise O
glorious Zion." Prayer by Mrs. What-
mough. Singing, "May we who know the
joyful sound." The Secretary being absent
Miss Hattie Woolly was appointed to act in
her place.
	Minutes of the last meeting were read
and accepted. Also minutes of the 10th
Ward Junior Association. President Mrs.
M. I. Horne observed; that it is gratifying
and interesting to hear the minutes of our
young ladies' meetings. They show, to us
that our efforts, made under the direction
of President Young, have not been lost. I
[new column]
was particularly impressed with the second
hymn which contained a line taken from
the words of the Savior, "Be ye not hearers
of the word alone but doers of it also." It
is by "doing" the things which we are com-
manded that we shall gain blessings. It
appears to me that the people are losing sight
of the object for which they came here, and
that the spirit of the world has taken
possession of them. Our religion is prac-
tical, and unless we obey the principles
contained therein we will not be able to
endure and overcome the  trials which we
must pass through in order to obtain celes-
tial glory. The Lord says, "He chasteneth
every son and daughter he receiveth." We
must not forget that our covenants are just
as binding upon us at the present time, as
they were when we first embraced the gos-
pel. Counselor Miss E. R. Snow said; I
will give a brief account of my visit south.
Mrs. Howard and myself have been visiting
the various Relief Societies of some of the
southern settlements. We had large and
attentive audiences composed of both ladies
and gentlemen. The first meeting we
attended was held last Thursday in Provo.
On Friday we met with the brethren and
sisters of Payson, the former requesting the
privilege of attending, and I really think
the brethren need quite as much instruction
in regard to the design of the Relief Socie-
ties as the sisters. Saturday and Sunday,
attended a general two-days meeting at
Salem. The following day we met the
brethren and sisters at a meeting held in
the Bowery of that place; Tuesday held
meeting at Springville; Wednesday met
with their President and Board at Bishop
Smoot's residence and spent the afternoon
in an organized meeting. Thursday we
visited the Relief Society at Pleasant Grove;
and on Friday attended meeting at Ameri-
can Fork. In all those places I spoke of
our young ladies' organizations and of their
success. I told them the minutes of their
meetings were instructive to the old as well
as to the young, and that in associating to-
gether they have obtained the Spirit of God
which enables them to understand as we do.
I urged the necessity of our young men be-
ing organized. The Bishops seemed to see
that it was necessary tha something should
be done towards their improvement. The
young women of Payson have an organiza-
tion and while there we appointed a good
motherly lady Superintendent to whom the
young ladies could refer for counsel. In
other places they seemed to realize the im-
portance of something being done to save
those born in Zion. I, like Sister Horne,
do not believe in enthusiasm but believe
in being wide awake Saints. I remarked
in Springville that I liked whole hearted
Saints—those who could forget their selfish
interests and seek only for the advancement
of the Kingdom of God. Would we but
look upon things as they truly exist we
should find there should be but one mutual,
general interest. In a city of twenty thou-
sand inhabitants, the majority of whom
profess to be Latter-day Saints this room
should be filled to overflowing. This organiza-
tion, in connection with the Relief Societies,
is one of the aids in assisting the Kingdom
of God to attain unto perfection, and it is a
shame that these meetings are not more
appreciated by the sisters.
	The sisters of the various Wards have
accomplished much but not one tenth of
of their duties has yet been performed.
These Societies in their organization, are
designed to elevate and purify women; to
make our drughters ornaments in Zion, in-
stead of being the trifling, good-for-nothing
specimens of human life that, in too many
instances we see in our midst; to look after


the poor and administer to their wants,
which is an angelic service, is only a tith-
ing of what should be the result of these
organizations. If the people understood
this subject as Joseph Smith did, the moral
character of every woman, who is not a
member of a R. Society would be questioned.
It is the privilege of woman to give charac-
ter to society, and she does do it. As sure
as the sisters arise and take hold of the
work, the brethren will wake up, because
they must be at the head, and instead of
devoting their attention to the gaining of
temporal wealth which is perishable, they
will seek first the Kingdom of God, and let
the Lord add his blessings which he has
promised. Counselor Zina D. Young said:
dear sisters we have been truly blessed 
this afternoon in hearing the words of life
and salvation; they are just as binding upon
us as though they had  been given by pro-
phets. Were it not for tradition many
would hail the sound of the Gospel as a
priceless boon. Woman has much to do;
how active we should be in seeking to carry
out the work which is before us. I feel
grateful to God that our lot is cast in this
land and in this time. We should consider
ourselves blessed above all other women in
the world. Mrs. Howard and Mrs. Pixton
made interesting remarks after which
Counselor Mrs. Woodruff said: I feel like
expressing my satisfaction with the remarks
we have heard. We enjoy many priveleges,
and I would that we could more fully appre-
ciate them. For one I am thankful to be
permitted to have a name among this people,
who are or should be a light to the world.
Counselor Mrs. S. Kimball remarked that;
I am pleased with the privilege of adding my
testimony to what has been said. If I may
select any subject which has been more in-
teresting to me than another it is that
touching upon the necessity of understand-
ing the organization of the Relief Societies.
I know of no Ward that properly sustains
them and if there is a remedy I don't know
where to find it. I have thought the sisters
were benefitted by meeting together and
interchanging ideas. Sister Horne once
suggested that we send missionaries to the
different Wards. Mrs. Mary. R. Thompson
observed, that she had always felt a deep
interest in the Relief Societies and was
pleased to hear so good a report from the
southern settlements. Mrs. Whatmough
spoke of the interest she had felt for the
daughters of Zion. The young ladies of the
19th Ward were once organized into a Re-
trenchment Society, but their President had
now left the city and for some time past
their meetings had been discontinued. She
felt very anxious to have them reorganized,
being willing to open her house for them to
hold meetings in, supplying lights and fire
during the winter. Counselor E. R. Snow
said: My sisters, here is an opening to do
a work of salvation. We all desire to do
good, but we have got to take some tangi-
ble points. It is good to fast and pray but
we must combine some particular acts with
our faith. In our efforts to produce a reform
among the young we met the greatest
opposition from those who should have been
saviors on Mount Zion. Let every mother
encourage the movement spoken of by Mrs. Wat-
mough. Our young people know nothing of the
power of the Gospel. In the early days of the
Church, children as well as parents possessed the
Spirit of God. Let every sister realize that she
is called to be a savior on Mount Zion, and en-
gaged in the work of salvation which is needed in
our midst every day. It does not do to only think,
feel, and talk but we have got to succeed in our
aims, and so sure as the young ladies become
Saints, the young men will exert themselves to be-
come Saints. I am thankful that we have one
organization of young men in our city. After short
and very interesting remarks from others...the
meeting adjourned for two weeks, after singing,
"Wake O wake the world from sleeping," and
benediction by Counselor Mrs. Zina D. Young.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
	Take things quietly, friends! and you
will thereby protect yourselves, and those
around you, from much unnecessary trouble.
Doubtless, there are many things in the
world calculated to try our temper, to per-
plex and torment us; but taking them
quietly is the best way of lessening their
power. If a man, carrying a basket of eggs
on his head, meets with an accident, where-
by the basket is overturned, and the eggs
are broken, will the stamping and storming,
his tearing and swearing, lessen his misfor-
tune, or recover one of his broken eggs?
Surely not; then, how vain is the indulgence
of his frenzy, and how much wiser would it
be to take things quietly! There are some
persons who can hardly live on earth unless
they are bouncing and bursting about like
so many squibs and crackers, and who, one
might imagine, would hardly think heaven
itself worth having, unless they went there
in a sky-rocket! Avoid such people as you
would a bomb-shell. What is the use of
going through the world like a volcano,
raging and roaring, spitting and spouting
fire in every direction, annoying others
and spending ourselves to no purpose?
	We have heard of the philosopher who
on observing a man frantic with grief, tear-
ing the hair from his head, exclaimed to
those around, "Doeth this man think that
baldness is a remedy for grief?" There was
much wisdom in the remark. If, by giving
way to passion or ungovernable grief, we
could thereby remove or lessen our afflic-
tions, there would be some sense in our
raving; but if, on the contrary, it invariably
increased our distress, why then, friends:
you will agree with us that it is much better
to take things quietly. By calmness and
self-possession our energies are rendered
doubly effective; and a man with an unruf-
fled spirit does that easily which under the
influence of passion he could not perform at
all.—"Miscellany."
	Seasoning Glass.—Place the glass in a
vessel of cold water and heat the water
gradually until it boils. Glasses of every
description thus prepared, it is said will
afterwards bear boiling water poured on
them without injury.
	Lemon Dumplings.—Mince four ounces
of suet, and mix it with the same quantity
of moist sugar, half a pound of bread-crumbs,
and the juice and peel of a lemon cut small.
Put them into tea cups and boil them three
quarters of an hour.
	Cleaning Stoves.—Stove luster, when
mixed with turpentine and applied in the
usual manner, is blacker, more glossy and
more durable than when mixed with any
other liquid. The turpentine prevents rust,
and when put on an old rusty stove will
make it look as good as new.
	Lemon Pie.—One tablespoonful of corn
starch, wet with a little cold water, stirred
into a cup of boiling water; pour it, boiling;
on a cup of sugar and a tablespoonful of
butter. After it cools, add the juice and
rind of one lemon and one beaten egg.
Bake with a upper  and lower crust.
	Keeping Honey.—To keep honey all the
year round, let it run through a fine sieve
to separate from particles of wax; then boil
t gently in an earthen vessel, skim off
the foam which gathers on the top, and
cool it in jars. After covering tightly set
them away in a cool cellar.
	Dr. S. S. Blookgett, ot Ogdenburgh, N.
Y., writing to the "Dental Cosmos," once
condemned the use of fine charcoal as a
tooth powder. He asserts that it is as
sharp as diamond dust, and soon wears off
the enamel. He says: "The great Denti-
frice that should be used at all times, and
under all circumstances, is soap. Its alka-
line properties serve to neutralize the acids
contained in the fluids of the mouth, and its
cleansing properties will correct the breath
and remove offensive odor sooner than any
article I have seen tried."
	Corn Soup.—Eight large ears, cut off the
grains and scrape the cobs. Cover this with
water (not too much) and boil until perfectly
done. Now add two quarts of milk, let it
come to a boil; stir in two tablespoonfuls of
butter rolled in a tablespoonful of flour, let
the whole boil a few minutes; have ready
the yolks of three eggs well beaten; pour the 
soup on them, stirring hard all the time.
Serve immediately after seasoning to taste.
	Plain Cake.—One cup of sugar, one
of cream, one egg, two and one half cups of
flour, one teaspoonful of soda, a little salt.
Any spice you like.
	Sugar Bread.—Three pounds of sugar,
one and one half of butter, six pounds of
flour, eight eggs, one ounce and three
coppers' weight of baking powder, and one
pint of sour milk. Mix not too stiff, and
bake in a moderate heat.
	Measure Cake.—Six cups of sifted
flour, four of powdered sugar, one cup of
sour cream, one cup of butter and five eggs.
Cream half the flour with the butter, the
other half sift into the eggs and sugar after
they have been well beaten together. Mix
well, and just before baking add the cream,
with one teaspoonful of soda and the juice
of one lemon. Bake quickly.


A FELLOW having been "sworn to the truth"
of a statement he had made, afterwards
confessed that the major part of it was false.
"Did you swear to the truth of it?" he was 
asked. "Yes," he replied, "but I did not
swear to the falsehood of it, and part of it 
was truth."


Scientific Pun. — A gentleman was 
showing a friend a balloon of ox bladder in-
flated with oxygen. "But," observed the 
friend, "if the oxygen should escape, how
can you get it into the bladder again?"
"That is not the difficulty," quoth a by-
stander, "it is not how to get the oxygen
into the bladder again, but how to get the
bladder into the ox again!"


ALMOND custards are thus made: Blanched
sweet almonds, four ounces; beat them to a
smooth paste in a mortar, and add it to one
pint of thick cream, with the yolks of four
eggs, two or three spoonfuls of rose water,
and two drops each of essential oil of al-
monds and essence of lemon. Stir the whole
over a slow fire, until of a proper consis-
tence, then pour it into cups. Some use
milk instead of cream, and two eggs in
addition to the above.


TEN parts of tin, combined with one hun-
dred of copper, form bronze, is the usual
composition for statues. Common bell-
mettle is composed of three parts of copper
and one of tin. For very small bells, a
small portion of zinc improves the tone.
Speculum metal for telescopes is composed
of equal parts of tin and copper.  It is very
white, very hard and close in grain, and
eceives an exquisite polish.

SPLINTERS.

	It is estimated that there are 125,000 wo-
men in New York City who earn their living.
.....The weekly wages of female teachers in
Maine is said to be but $3:50. How do
they live?..........There were one hundred
and forty-two babies born in San Francisco
on the 2nd inst..........The "Tulare Times"
describes a wild man seen in the range of
the Sierras, as of being of gigantic stature,
a white man who goes entirely without
clothes and is covered all over with long
black hair. The Indians think it is the
devil or ghost of some wicked dead man......
A little girl in St. Louis lately obtained
access to a bottle of corrosive sublimate
which she drank and died......A Missouri
colored man, named Messenger, being in-
toxicated, a few evenings ago, shot another
colored man, killing him instantly......The
colored people of Ohio want more extensive
political recognition, and are considering
how best to secure it.....Kildare, Ireland,
has had a little disturbance from English
rifles......Mr. Allsop, the son of the cele-
brated brewer, has made a successful run
for the Parliamentary seat for East Stafford-
shire, England......The Count de Chambord 
says France is weary of agitation, and would
be saved and rise from her ruins stronger
and greater than ever, if the real conditions
of safety were rightly understood......The 
Soliders' Orphans Home, of York State, has
a semi-annual appropriation of $2,500......
The hog cholera has made some farmers in
Springfield, Illinois, poorer by several hun-
dred dollars in a few days time this season.
......An English newspaper, established in
1723, recently celebrated its 150th birth-day
by republishing a fac-simile of its first issue.
......The late rain storms in Pioche have
caused much interruption to travel and ren-
der important repairs constantly necessary.
..........A late sensational snake story is of a
reptile 4 inches long, of a greenish color,
which was found in an egg at South Bend.
......An innocent father in Milwaukee, Wis-
consin, carries love notes to and from a clerk
in his office for his daughter, whose com-
pany has been forbidden to the young man;
the funniest part of the joke being that the
father knows nothing of the correspondence,
while the news papers have found it out......
Four miners were occupying a cabin in
Pioche, which was struck by lightning; one
of the men was lamed in both legs; another
had one leg paralized and was struck deaf.
......An Alabama man captured and caged
an owl which its mate came nightly to feed
until it was also captured..A little boy in one
of the Southern settlements was chasing a
rabbit when it entered a hole in the earth,
into which the boy thrust his arm and
brought out a large rattle snake......Prince
Napoleon has been in Corsica and has taken
part in the deliberations of the Council
General of Ajacio......William M. Meredith,
one of Pennsylvania's most eminent lawyers,
recently died aged seventy-seven years......
Clemont County, Ohio, has a man living
who is one hundred and ten years old,
named Elijah Newton......The constables
lately got after Rev. W. H. H. Murray in
the Adirondacks, for killing deer, contrary
to the game laws of the State of New York.
.......A residence in the neighborhood of
Sandfordville was lately robbed and two
Poughkeepsie editors, who had gone there
for a breath of fresh air, were put under
surveillance as burglars......A druggist who
served in the army of Napoleon I, named
Elias Durand, recently died in Philadelphia.
......New York imported 142,877 barrels of
pork, 145,465,447 pounds of lard, 275,306,-
308 pounds of ham, between the 1st of
November, 1872, and the 15th of August,
1873......Virginia exhibits considerable ex-
citement over her "Black Man[-]s Party,"
who are trying hard to over-rule the whites."
A train on an Ohio railroad recently ran
over a farmer's wagon which contained
himself, his wife and two daughters; the
children were killed on the spot and the
mother died from the injuries received......
Terrible storms and lightning are prevalent
this year......A wet winter is predicted by
California conjurers........The Mason and
Hamblin organs got the "First Medal" at
the Vienna Exposition..........The "Daily
Graphic" suggests that "Judges sent to Utah
should first pass a competitive examination
in morals" preferring one who denounces
polygamy but practices bigamy.........The
Grand Central Hotel, in Omaha, is now
called the Pullman House in honor of Mr.
G. M. Pullman, of Palace car fame......
Twelve men were killed in Lincoln mine,
Sutler Creek, California on the 5th inst........
Portland is excited about the increase of
small-pox......Pioche is not as safe as it was
for villains since the late trials and convic-
tion of some of them......The dining table
of the Emperor of Russia is so arranged
that at the touch of a signal like the rubbing
of Aladdin's lamp, down goes the table
through the floor and a new table, loaded
with fresh dishes and viands, rises in its
place......It is estimated that the extent of
new railways built in this country in 1873
will be more than forty per cent. less than
for 1872......A national convention of the
different Italian societies assembled at St.
Louis, Missouri, Aug. 27th......The women
in Ohio want to vote on the new Constitu-
tion........Miss H. Chamberlain has been
elected Principal of the Newark (Del.)
Academy in the place of Prof. Porter who
resigned......A woman lately won her first
case as a lawyer in Chicago.


TO THE LADIES.

	The Thirteenth Ward Assembly Rooms have
been secured again this season for the forth-
coming fair. The following notice should be
carefully observed by all whom it may concern:
	TO THE RELIEF SOCIETIES.—"Ladies"––The
Deseret Agricultural and Manufacturing Society
in last Spring's circular, invited your honorable
Societies to the forthcoming Fair, which will
be held on the 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 6th, 7th and 8th
days of October next.
	It is desirable that this the eleventh exhibi-
tion should be a true exposition of the indus-
tries of Utah, and for this purpose it has been
deemed proper by the Board of Directors that
the Relief Societies should be again called upon
to respond as far as their circumstances will
permit.
	The Board express their gratitude for the
interest taken heretofore and hope that the
Exhibition of ’73 will witness a crowning effort
in the contributions of the Relief Societies.
	By order of the board of directors.
		ROBERT. L. CAMPBELL, Secretary.


USEFUL STUDY

	It is a pleasing feature in the course of events
that those studies which have long been 
thought of most importance in the education of 
girls, are beginnig to be viewed more exten-
sively in their true light. That is, that al-
though beautiful as ornamental accomplish-
ments, they are not the most essential to life
and happiness. Young women have expended
unmeasured time and means in acquiring fam-
iliarity with arts and sciences suited only to
the embellishment of life without understanding
any principle connected with the subject upon
which depends the exercise and enjoyment of
all else they learn. They have pursued all
other branches of knowledge, giving the least,
if any attention to the laws of health and the
years in learning to sing, neglecting to give one
hour's study to those wonderful instruments,
the ear and lungs.
	There is no other subject upon which, com-
paratively speaking, people are so generally
ignorant as that one which is of greatest im-
portance to their temporal well-being. Yet,
once interested in the study of physiology,
there is none other more charming and elevat-
ing in its nature than this when rightly
pursued.
	The strangely complicated machinery of 
which the human body is composed, with its
intricate and perfect working, is far more
curious than anything the ingenuity of man
could invent; or even comprehend. Yet,
throught the providence of an All-wise Creator,
and by cultivation, enough understanding and
intelligence may be obtained, concerning this
most marvelous piece of workmanship, to in-
sure safety to its various members in passing
through the ordinary vicissitudes of life. And is
there not infinitely more wisdom in procuring
a knowledge of those divine laws by which life
may not only be prolonged, but the enjoyment
of it, and its usefulness increased, than in 
studying ought else to their neglect?
	It is often through ignorance of these laws
that diseases are entailed which prove incura-
ble and terminate fatally or make miserable
and comparatively useless life which might
have been joyous and eminently valuable.
Knowledge which may serve to prevent such
mistakes, cannot be looked upon as superficial
to improfessional readers. It is much cheaper
and safer to take measures of preserving
health, than to trust to means of redeeming it
when lost. But unless it is known what will
and what will not be injurious to the system,
how is a sure course to be adopted to prevent
mistakes in life which may peril and injure
the delicate and wonderful human structure?
The means for obtaining an understanding of
self, of the errors to be avoided, and laws of
life to be observed, so as to prevent disease, to
remove or retain health and vigor, are more
numerous than formerly and more within the
reach of the people! We consider that the
acquiring of such knowledge should be made a
principal feature in our common and high
schools; and would recommend all, so far as
possible, to acquire a correct understanding of 
the organization, laws and needs of their being.
And to seek for the power of self-control that 
they may make use of this understanding for
their best good.
	Considering the great importance of this sub-
ject, especially to women, we thus earnestly
seek to impress it upon their attention; fully 
believing that, in a general sense, there is no
one study so inestimably useful and requisite
to all persons, young and old, of both sexes, as 
that of self.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


POLYGAMY.

	Writing upon this subject, Mrs. Dumway, 
edeitor of the "New North West," hopes to
hear from us "when Mrs. 2nd comes along."
She also ventures that we "will then desire an
interview with Judge McKean."
   We presume the lady is very sincere in her
manner of viewing things. But sincerity in
belief is no proof of correctness.
	If we were wanting to be divorced, we would
be likely to appeal to some one who could
accomplish the matter in a more satisfactory
way, and with less noise than our judges are
in the habit of doing; thinking the instructions
of ancient Paul still worthy of consideration,
in which he says to the Corinthians: "Dare
any of you having a matter against another,
go to law before the unjust, and not before the
saints?"
	Mrs. D. makes farther suggestions, which we
take in good part, as they were intended, and 
will endeavor to meet fairly and honestly. 
She says: 
   "We are not of those who rank the polygamic
women of Utah with adulterers. From their
stand-point they are remarkably virtuous, yet
our short stay of one week among them, while
it endeared us to many for their noble heroism
and genuine hospitality and goodness of heart,
revealed to us in individual cases such poig-
nancy of grief, especially among first wives,
whose husbands had found other loves and
founded other families, as we hope never again
to meet anywhere.  As we write there rises up
before our mental vision the pale image of one
of these victims of the lusts of men, miscalled
religion, whose earnest appeal through us to
her relatives in Portland, for means to get
"anywhere out of Utah," that she might be
able to get her daughters away from the blight 
of polygamy, haunts us yet. Our Utah friend
is a very valuable 'Exponent' of the equal
political rights of women.  Will she be kind
enough to tell why, if polygamy is right for
men, that it is a one-sided institution? Why
is it not as necessary for wives to have more 
than one husband, as for husbands to have 
more than one wife?"
   If "individual cases" may be taken as a stand-
ard by which a people and their principles are
to be judged, it is but necessary to glance
at the news columns of a daily paper to find
sufficient evidence to condemn the world with
no exceptions.
	Are those sympathies which are so accute
when awakened in behalf of polygamic wives,
less alive to the griefs of such women as Kate
Stoddard, Mrs. James Fisk, Mrs. Critteuden 
and thousands of others, the records of whose
wrongs without redress are daily published to
the world, while yet not a fourth is told?
	We wonder if  one pale victim's relatives 
in Portland were ever searched out, and if they
sent her means to get herself and daughters out
of Utah with. We hope they did; for that, 
indeed, would look like sympathy with som-
substance to it.
	Now we are requested to tell why if polgamy
is right for man, it is not right for woman in
the same way.
	Without consulting Christian views in the
least, a little sound reflection upon the laws of 
nature, should be a sufficient reply to such a
question. But taken into a religous considera-
tion, we may answer by asking, when Laban
gave Jacob his second daughter, Rachel, why
did he not furnish Leah with another husband?
We take Mrs. Duniway to be a Christian, and
suppose she believes and understand her Bible.
	We regret that our limited space will not
permit of farther expression now, but we may
refer to the subject again.


IN France there are in use half a million
donkeys, and in Italy 400,000. These hardy
little beasts do all the hardest of the work.


THE BETTER WAY.

	The way in which many "woman suffragists"
bandy the never ceasing and still unanswerable
question of "woman's rights" by depicting
woman's universal wrongs in such a light as
even to seriously prevent the obtaining of her
undisputed natural rights is simply absurd
and ridiculous. Often it is seemingly made to
appear that all women are subjected to tyranny
and degradation because some of them are not
allowed to advance as far and  fast as they
would, and might do, if all viewed their cause
correctly. And from the evidence of some
writers and speakers it would seem that because
foolish and wicked men are to be found, all men
are, in their estimation, fools or rascals.
	Why should the vindicating of so just, and
reasonable an object as universal suffrage for
women be carried to such extremes by its over-
zealous and impatient friends as to disgust men
of sense and make women of refined natures
almost ashamed to vote when lawfully privi-
leged to do so?
	Would it not be wisdom to be more just,
moderate and discriminating in advocating
the rights of woman, when her interests are so
closely enwoven with those of man that they
cannot be separated; while seeking to assume
rights not yet granted to make better use of
those already in their possession? Thus prov-
ing that they not only want their rights but are
as able to appreciate and use them wisely as
are the stronger sex.


﻿			HOME AFFAIRS.

	The Sabbath Schools of Cache county are
preparing to have a grand jubilee at Logan
City.
	The Foundation is being laid for a fine illu-
minated clock, which is to grace the corner of
the Eagle Emporium.
	Madam De Barry has been entertaining
some of the lovers of music in this city with a
charming performance on the piano. Her
musical abilities are said to be excellent.
	A company of young men in the 6th Ward,
have formed themselves into a Mutual Improv-
ment association. It will be well if their
example is followed by many others; and if
rightly conducted, much general good will be
the result.
	On Monday evening, the 1st inst., the Sab-
bath Schools in this county were well repre-
sented at the monthly Convention. The
subject which received most attention was
that of the forth coming Mammoth Concert, the
exact time of which is not yet decided.
	The late visits and missionary labors of
President Brigham Young and party, through
the settlements of Bear Lake and Cache val-
leys, have been of a cheering and refreshing
nature to all concerned. The company returned
to this city on the 6th inst., according to ex-
pectations; all in good health and spirits.
	Work on the Utah Northern Rail Road is
being pushed ahead by the enterprising men of
Cache valley. Excellent grain crops have been
raised in that county this season. But the
people say the greatest difficulty is that there
is no "cash" in "Cache," and they find it rather
embarrassing at times, to have cash accounts to
settle.
	The Salt Lake Theatre has been undergoing
some material changes of late,, under the direc-
tions of its new proprietors, Messrs. H. B.
Clawson, John T. Caine, Joseph A. Young,
Thomas Williams and Le Grande Young,
who purchased it a short time ago, of President
Brigham Young. Many improvements are
being made in the interior of the house, which
is designed to open October 1st.
	There is considerable novelty in sitting
opposite some "strangers" on the cars, and
hearing them talk of what they have seen
during their short stay in Utah. A lady of this
city happened to be thus entertained while
traveling between Brigham City and Ogden a
few days since. It was a well known gentle-
man of Brigham City, who was being described
and the lady viewed the description as a very
flattering one. The stranger who had visited him
declared him to be fifty years old, and the halest,
heartiest man of that age, he had ever known;
adding very suggestively that he was the hus-
band of three wives and the father of twenty-two
children, the youngest of which was ten months
old and his seventeenth boy. The children
were as fine, rosy and nice as he had ever seen
—and it was repeated—the "Colonel" was such
a jolly old soul, as robust and well as any in 
the country.
	Miss Eliza R. Snow and the editor of the
EXPONENT visited Cache valley last week, and
met with the Relief Societies of several of the
principal towns; the object of the visit
being to circulate particular information con-
cerning the two classes being organized in this
city, for the purpose of preparing women phy-
sicians. They were kindly welcomed by
hospitable friends wherever they visited, and
large congregations assembled to listen to the
instructions given; which were varied and
interesting. Miss Snow was warmly congratu-
lated on having so successfully performed her
tour on the Continent and through the Holy
Land, of which she conversed with much ease
and satisfaction. The only inconvenience which
threatened the ladies, during a week's absence
from home, was an appearance of their being
obliged to "ride on a rail" from Brigham
Junction to Coriune. And even this chance for
an adventure was over-ruled by the timely
interference of Mr. Oliver Snow, who appeared
on the scene with horses and carriage. Miss
Snow admits that although very polite and
gentlemanly railroad men are to be found else-
where, those of Utah, surpass all others with
whom she has met. On the evening of the 9th
inst., she and her companion greatly enjoyed
a rich treat of very sweet singing from the
Wellsville choir, by whom they were seren-
aded.


UNHAPPY HUSBAND.

	Perhaps it is your own fault. Too many
men take all the love their wives can
give them as a matter of course, never
thinking to return it till the wife gets tired
of looking for it and ceases to bestow affec-
tion. Court your wife, just as you did in
your wooing. It won't hurt you, and de-
pend upon it, you can win her back in no
other way :– We have known husbands to
never once bestow a word or look of love
for years, and when at last their disap-
pointed wives are caught gaily laughing and
chatting with other company in the very
vein of humor which first enchained these
exacting lords, they have become as cross
and repellant as hornet-stung bears. Don't
be such a noodle, we beg. Women are not
angels, and they can't always endure every-
thing. A man is foolish who expects to
retain the warm, girlish affections of his
wife by indifference when he gained them
only by most attentive wooing. We repeat,
court her. Let her see that you care for
her, that her comfort is essential to your
enjoyment, and if she don't forgive and love
and bless you we know nothing about
women, that's all.—"New North West."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[For the Exponent.
			MY TREASURES.

	My Mountain Home, dear, honored place,
	  My heart doth fondly love thee;
	I love the light of nature's face
	  Dispensed around, above me.

	The snow hills sunk to murmuring floods,
	  The grave, surrounding towers,
	A floating field of gorgeous clouds,
	  And slopes of waving flowers.

	Ye all have notes that reach my ear,
	   With soft, mellifluous wooing.
	But through my spirit's silent sphere
	  Is music deeper flowing.

	Eternal truth has waked my soul,
	  And chants with sacred pleasure,
	Though nature's charms rare gifts unroll,
	  Faith is my holiest treasure.
									KENT.


AN ADDRESS.
		By Miss Eliza R. Snow.
Delivered in the Tabernacle. Ogden, at a Relief
	Society meeting, Thursday afternoon, August
	14th, 1873.
		Reported by James Taylor.
	[As the first part of the address is of a
descriptive nature, in which the speaker
delineates the course of travel pursued by
President George A. Smith and party, the
lately returned tourists, and as the readers
of the Exponent have been favored with
much the same details through Miss Snow's
letters, we merely make a few extracts, giv-
ing the latter portion of the address in full.]
—Ed.
		In Lyons, we saw them weave silk
of various descriptions. They weave pho-
tograph likenesses and battle scenes, and
delineate countenances plainly as if they
had been painted.
	In the King's Palace in Munich, I was
shown window curtains made of glass, in
brocade and beautiful colors. Without feel-
ing of them, I should not have thought but
that they were silk, but they were glass.
		I visited many churches and cathe-
drals; I visited mosques (a mosque is a 
Mahamedan place of worship;) saw all I
could under the governments where we
traveled. And the more I saw, the more I
felt the necessity of the Lord! having a
government on the earth for the sake of the
people, and thought how well off the Saints
in Zion were; and what blessings they en-
joyed. Blessings and privileges above those
of all the nations of the earth. And, we my
sisters, we have the privilege of being
organized according to the pattern which
God has given for the females in the Church
of Jesus Christ. We are privileged above
all other woman kind on the face of the
earth. I thought when I was abroad, I
thought before I went, and I have thought
much since I returned, how necessary for
the Saints of the living God to be more of a
distinct people than what they are, to be
the Saints of God in very deed––and to be
as different from the rest of the world as
our privileges are more exalted—we should
be a shining light to  the nations of the earth.
But I often say to myself, are we what we
should be?
	I was talking with President Young about
how we were received abroad. He said
that the Saints were growing in influence;
and that if they lived up to their privileges,
their influence would be far greater than
it is now. I told him that I believed that
if we were as distinct a people as the Lord
wanted us to be our influence would be far
greater. He said it would. But instead of
that, are we not trying to gain influence by
assimilating to the habits and customs, and
the spirit of the world?
[new column]
	Now I do not know that this would apply
to my sisters in ogden, for they are a live
people. Their works exhibit that. And 
their reputation bespeaks them to be awake
to their duties. But still we are never to
come to a stand point. We are to be pro-
gressing and growing better. If we have
done well to-day, we must do still better to-
morrow. We believe in eternal pro-
gressing, and growing better. If we have
done well to-day, we must do still better to-
morrow. We believe in eternal progression.
It will not do to say that we have so much
to do that we cannot do any more, because
the works and duties for women in Zion are
constantly increasing. No where on the
earth has woman so broad a sphere of labor
and duty, of responsibility and action, as in
Utah.
	I tried in many instances to make
people believe that women in Utah had
more freedom than women had any where
else on the earth. But they thought that
they knew better than I did. I told them 
how long I had lived with the people, and
that I ought to know better than strangers.
	To be sure we have trials, but what are
they? I want to ask my sisters now a seri-
ous question. When you are filled with the
Spirit of God, and the Holy Ghost rests upon
you—that comforter which Jesus promised
and which takes of the things of God and
gives them to us, and shows us things
to come, and brings all things to our re-
membrance—when you are filled with this
spirit, do you have any trials? I do not
think you do. For that satisfies and fills
up every longing of the human heart, and
fills up every vacuum. When I am filled 
with that spirit, my soul is satisfied; and I
can say in good earnest, that the trifling
things of the day do not seem to stand in
my way at all. But just let me loose my
hold of that spirit and power of the
Gospel, and partake of the spirit of the
world, in the slightest degree, and trouble
comes; there is something wrong. I am
tried; and what will comfort me? You can-
not impart comfort to me that will satisfy
the Immortal mind, but that which comes
from the fountain above. And is it not our
privilege to so live that we can have this
constantly flowing into our souls? To be
sure we have many of the crosses of life,
but what do we meet them for? Are they
for our own good and benefit or do we meet
them all as for Zion's sake? Do we let Zion
take full possession of our desires, our am-
bition?
	Now my sisters, though I took that tour to
Europe and Asia, would I have gone to
gratify my own personal selfishness? I felt
that it was right for me to go. My sisters
sent me. I went believing that good would
result from my going. And I have had
that belief all the time. I never once
regretted starting. I told President Smith
that I did not think that anything could
occur to make me regret that I went. I
try never to allow myself to do anything for
a selfish gratification. Still we are all frail
and weak mortals of the earth, earthy:
But that comforter, which is a legacy that
belongs to every Saint of God, and to
every one who has been baptized for the
remission of sins, will lift our desires to a
nobler aim, and we forget all about self.
We have self all absorbed in the interests of
the work of God. We are here to perform
duties, and to do our part towards establish-
ing God's Kingdom. We, my sisters, have
as much to do as our brethren have. We are
to work in union with them. Every woman
who fills her position as a wife, honorably,
stands as a counselor to her husband. Not
a dictator, a counselor. And what a life it
is to live my sisters! What a noble life,
to live, so as to fill this position, in which
we are blessed, and are honorable as women
of God.
	Paul the Apostle anciently spoke of holy
women. It is the duty of each one of us to
be a holy woman. We shall have elevated
aims, if we are holy women. We shall feel
that we are called to perform important
duties. No one is exempt from them.
There is no sister so isolated, and her
sphere so narrow but what she can do a
great deal towards establishing the King-
dom of God upon the earth.	I am proud to see so many young ladies
associated with the Relief Society in Ogden.
There should be an association in every
settlement, and in every Ward in Salt Lake
City. And wherever there is a Relief
Society, the girls and young ladies should
have as much to do as other women already
in the Retrenchment Association. You
need not be startled at the words Retrenchment
Association. If you associate together,
your minds are improved, you are gaining
intelligence, and you are retrenching from
ignorance. The Spirit of God will impart
instruction to your minds, and you will
impart it to each other. I say, God bless
you my young sisters. Remember that you
are Saints of God; and that you have im-
portant works to perform in Zion.
	How is it with a great many of our young
people? The are baptized when they are
eight years old, and have hands laid upon 
them for the gift of the Holy Ghost. Do
they know what the Holy Ghost is? Do
they ever minister in the Holy Ghost?
Have they ever had the gifts of the Gospel?
Look around you and think how many there
are, children of the Latter-day Saints, who
know no more of the Gospel of Jesus Christ
than the heathens do. Why? because they
have had nothing to do with it. Their
fathers and mothers are good Saints. But
they are children of Zion. They can go
their own way, and do as they list. They
can be full of the spirit of the gentiles, and
be so blind that they cannot see the differ-
ence between the profession of a gentile and
the sincerity of the Saints. They have no
light in them. Why is it? Was it so at
the beginning of this Church? No it was
not. I can bear testimony that in the
beginning of the Church, children and young
people had the gifts of the Gospel, and prayed
and sang. They talked as much as old
people. They knew what the Spirit of God
was. There was no need of cautioning
them about the spirit of the world. They
had that monitor within them, the Holy
Ghost which showed them things in their
true light. I should like to see the effects
of it now in our young people. There is
some cause why it is not so. And I think
it would amount to quite as much, and a
little more, to have attention paid to these
things in such a manner that it would save
the children that are born in Zion, than it
would to go to the trouble of raising money,
and bringing the Saints from the nations of
the earth. Why are there not some steps
taken to save the youth?
	Now, in Salt Lake City President Young,
counseled Sister Horne and myself, and
Sister Smoot to organize the Young girls.
He wanted his young girls organized first.
He dictated who should be president, and
she to choose her Counselors, and start out
right. But how did it work? There was
cold water thrown on the efforts made. It
was turned to ridicule by many. They said
"what is the use of the girls meeting to-
gether, and praying, and talking?" And I
would ask what is the use of our brethren
meeting together? If the young girls and
the boys do not need the Spirit of the Gos-
pel, there does not any person need it.


But, I think the state of society in Salt Lake
City does  need it. You may talk to the
young about the follies of the world and the
unholy influences of the gentiles till dooms
day, and it will make no impression. But,
you place them in a position where they
will get the Holy Ghost, and that will be a
sure protection against outside influences.
Some will say, “our-boys, are sent out on
missions and then they get the Spirit of
God." Some of the boys are sent on mis-
sions, and it is necessary to keep them out,
that they may retain the Spirit of God, if
there is not enough of it at home. Some
of our young men who are sent out on
missions are obliged to seek to God but
when they come home they are laughed out
of it, and perhaps fall into the practices
of those who are here! It is not so with all
of them to be sure.
	In referring again to the organization of
the young ladies, I may say we have aposta-
tized in Salt Lake City, but there are so
many left as to show what that organiza-
tion would have been if carried out as
President Young desired it to be. We have
organizations of young ladiea in Salt Lake
City, who do not need anybody to tell them
about the influences and fashions of the 
world. They have just as much distaste for
them as the older folks have....I do think
to-day that if that counsel had been carried
out among the young girls, and boys, and
they had been organized and got the Spirit
of God, that our society would have shown
a very different character from what it does.
Some of the mothers and fathers, who spoke
lightly of it, now see the necessity of it,
to their sorrow.
	"Well, but now, Sister Eliza, are you not
scolding folks?" I have to talk so loud, as I 
want you all to hear, and it may seem like
scolding.
	There are a great many good things that
I could talk about, but I want to talk about
things that will produce effects, and effects
of salvation. It does not do to fool away
our time, thinking that all is well. It is
necessary for us to be laying that founda-
tion, which will secure the salvation of the
young, as well as of the old, and not leave
it to the exigencies of time, nor to the
chances of circumstances, but each one of us
see to it ourselves, and cultivate those
principles that will tend to salvation.
	Now, I am thankful that these my young
sisters meet together. You will be blest.
Sister Richards will be blest for promoting
these organizations. I understand you are
in connection with the Relief Society. It
makes no difference. But I want to say
one or two things, that I wish you to
remember. It is not only necessary for you
to meet together, you should talk to each
other, and talk of the Gospel of Jesus Christ.
You can talk of your every-day concerns,
but accustom yourselves to talking to each
other on the principles of the Gospel. It
will lay a foundation for your being emi-
nently useful hereafter. That is what you
are born for. You might as well have been
born in some other nation or dispensation
unless you can feel that you have a mission
in Zion. Study the principles of the Gospel,
converse on them, understand them, so that
you may be able to cope with any of the
wisest of the world. They are coming
here. They often ask questions of the
young, when they would not interrogate
the older ones. You want to understand
that you are living to be Saints, and never
be ashamed to acknowledge yourselves to
be Latter-day Saints. It is not enough that
you are born in Zion. It is true that the
Prophets said it should be an honor to be
born in Zion; but it is a dishonor not to live
up to the blessings of the Gospel to which
you are heirs.
	I see the Relief Society here has no house
of its own-no house that it can control. I
know the sisters are accomodated. But
each Society needs a house at its own dis-
posal. And if the sisters in Ogden, with 
the sanction of the brethren, undertake to
build a house, the brethren will help them.
It is said that God helps those who help
themselves, land the brethern are so god-
like that they will help you. Bishop Wooley
ley is now stepping forward to have such a
house built in the 13th Ward, in Salt Lake
City.
	There is another thing I want to mention
before I sit down. President Young is
requiring the sisters to get students of
Medicine. He wants a good many to get a
clasical education; and then get a degree
for Medicine. So far as getting the degree
is concerned, there would be no advantage,
but in connection with the degree, the
female practitioner stands on the same
grounds a man does. Are there here, now,
any sisters who have ambition enough, and
who realize the necessity of it, for Zion's
sake, to take up this study. There are
some who are naturally inclined to be
nurses; and such ones would do well to 
study Medicine, if they are inclined to do
so. If they cannot meet their own ex-
penses, we have means of doing so. It is
proposed that the sisters, instead of expend-
ing means to emigrate foreign Saints, spend
that means in educating young women.
Those who go through this course should be
young women. We have, in Salt Lake
City, a Mrs. Barker, who proposes to teach.
But there are many branches you need to 
study before going to the expense of being
boarded abroad to study. You need to study
Physiology, Anatomy, and other kindred
branches. 
	Then, another class of women is wanted
more advanced  in age, who are natural
nurses, and would be willing to study ob-
stetrics; this lady is going to give a series
of lectures for their benefit. If some who
have natural inclinations for nursing would
come and attend these lectures, that would
be fulfilling the requirements, so far. We
are waiting to get up as large a class as we
can. There are some eight or twelve now.
President Young said that he wanted one,
at least, from every settlement. Of course, 
so large a city as Ogden, should furnish
quite a number; so that we can have our
own practitioners, instead of having gentle-
men practitioners. In ancient times we
know that women officiated in this depart-
ment, and why should it not be so now?
The difficulty is in getting the sisters to feel
like undertaking it.
	Now if there are any who will attend
through all of these classes, their expenses
will be met if they are not able to meet
them. Several Ogden women should attend
this course  lectures, and confine themselves
to that particular department.
	We have to get up these classes and 
attend toall these things. Don't you see
that our sphere is increasing? Our sphere
of action will continually widen, and no
woman in Zion need to mourn because her
sphere is too narrow.
	God bless you, my sisters, and encourage
you, that you may be filled with light, and
realize that you have no interests but in the
welfare of Zion. Let your first business be
to perform your duties at home. But inas-
much as you are wise stewards, you will
find time for social duties, because these are
incumbent upon us as daughtes and mothers
in Zion. By seeking to perform every duty
you will find that your capacity will increase,
and you will be astonished at what you can
accomplish. You have been astonished
at what duties you have done. The Lord 
help us. The Lord is with His Saints and 
helps them to do His will and He watches
over them by night and by day. Inasmuch
as we continue faithful, we shall be those
that will be crowned in the presence of God
and the lamb. You, my sisters, if you are
faithful will become Queens of Queens,
and Priestesses unto the Most High God.
These are your callings. We have only to
discharge our duties. By and by our labors
will be past, and our names will be crowned
with everlasting honor, and be had in ever-
lasting remembrance among the Saints of
the Most High God.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WHO'S AHEAD

	A gentleman asks the girls the following
questions: “Could you love a man who wore
false hair when he had enough of his own?
Who painted his face and improved his form
as you improve (?) yours? Who pinched
his feet with small shoes, hands with small
gloves, waist with corsets; and then, as if
he had not already deformed himself
enough, tied a huge bustle to his back and
thrust tiny mountains of wire into his
bosom?" In reply to which, a lady re-
sponds: "Could you love a girl who defiled
her mouth with tobacco, and loaded the air
with fumes of cigars? Who staggered home
several times a week the worse for liquor?
Who indulged in fast horses, bet high at
races, and swaggered round the streets with
questionable companions? Which picture
wears the most alluring colors? — "Woman's
Journal."


GEORGE ELLIOT.
	Mrs. George H. Lewes (better known as
George Elliot), formerly Miss Evans, author
of "Adam Bede,' "Middlemarch," and
several other novels, was the daughter of a
poor clergyman, but in early life was adop-
ted by a wealthy gentleman, who gave her
the best education that England could afford.
When she left school, which happened when
she was still very young, Hubert Spencer
became her tutor and friend, and under his
skillful training her mind was developed
rapidly and broadly. He taught her to
think, to reflect, and to reason by scientific
methods. It is said he first advised her to
undertake the writing of works of fiction.
She applied herself to the languages, master-
ing German, French and Italian, and be-
came an accomplished musician, familiariz-
ing herself not only with the fine arts, but
also with metaphysics and logic. While in
this stage she translated, among other
things, Strauss' 'Life of Jesus,' and soon
after became one of the Westminster Review
writers. Here, by her intimacy with John
Stuart Mill and others, she became con-
firmed in their peculiar religious views.
Later on she married Professor Lewes,
under circumstances which are not nuder-
stood, and which are said to have evolved
a great deal of courage on her part. She
and her husband are zealous diciples of
Comte. Mrs. Lewes seldom appears in
society, and resides with her husband in a
retired nook in the north of London, hard
by Regent's Park, in a neighborhood of
pretty semi-suburban villas, her house being
surrounded by a neat garden and tastefully
arranged shrubbery, where the noise and
hum of the street is shut out by a high
wall.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿[For the Woman’s Exponent.
	A FAMILY CONVERSATION.

	Husband. Wife, have you read what the
papers say about Miss Smiley of Chicago
making proselytes by preaching? What
do you think women will do next? 
	Wife. Why, go to pleading law, I guess.
	Husband. Well, well! If anything on
earth can disgust me with women, it will
be to see them take the stand and go to
making speeches. I am sorry  it comes in
my day. I love woman in her own sphere.
	Wife. Pray tell me, what is her sphere?
Not to know anything only what her husband
tells her I suppose. I should be sorry to see
any of my girls turn lawyers; but if they
did, they would be true to their clients, and
not be bought and sold as some men are.
	Husband. Just as I expected; you bring
up argument and reason to sustain women
in their wild goose chase after law and phy-
sic and many other absurdities!
	Wife. Well Mr. Brown, you know that
my opportunities have been limited, that I
know but little, and am dependent upon
you for that. I have taken the Apostle
Paul's advice; "Let women learn from their
own husbands at home and be in subjection."
	Husband. Well, Mary, you've acted
wisely by staying at home and attending to
your domestic duties, and letting the out-
side world alone. Women are too pure to
mix up in political strife.
	Wife. Oh! that just reminds me of what
John asked me yesterday. He said, "Mother,
Judge Johnson is in the parlor talking to
father about that suit pending in court.
"What did he say about it?" "He said he
feared they would swindle the widow and
children out of the most of the property.
I could not understand half the judge said."
"Why not my son?" "Because I have never
read law, neither been in a court room, what
do they mean by mittimus and venire?"
"You'll have to ask your father, my son."
"Well mother, I think it is time we were
learning something. Suppose father had
dropped off suddenly like Mr. Grey, and
left his business in the condition it is in,
with those securities hanging over it?"
	Husband. Who ever would have thought
that John, no older than he is, would have
noticed what the Judge was talking about?
I believe I will bring home some law books
and have him go to reading them. Wife.
And Annie too; I don't wish to raise her as
ignorant as I am. We do not know whose
hands she may fall into when we are gone.
	Husband. Oh! Mary, you know I don't
want my daughters contaminated with law
and politics; they have enough to do to
study music and dress. Whenever women
engage in masculine pursuits, it takes away
all other charms.
	Wife. Well, I have found out that
charme will not take a virtuous woman
through the world. Look at your sister
Maud, brought up to know nothing but
to dress and to thump on the piano! She is
a sample of what charms will do towards
rearing a family. She married a man who
has spent all the property her father gave
her, and you went his security for five
thousand dollars. Now he has run away to
get rid of paying his debts and left her
with six little children, broken hearted,
and knows no more about feeling the re-
sponsibilities of wife and mother than our
little Jane.
	Husband. Oh well, you know if women
get good husbands they have no need to
learn anything outside the sphere of women.
	Wife. Why I heard you say last week,
that the heads of the nation were becoming
so corrupt, and dishonorable that they would
be the downfall of the nation; a great num-
ber of men who go to the Senate and House
of Representatives to represent the people
of this once Great Republic, can be bought
and sold for a few thousand dollars. If this
is the case, it is certainly time women were
beginning to study as least the first "Law"
of nature—self-preservation.
							W. E.


FACTS AND FIGURES.
	The total length of railroads in Germany,
at the close of 1859, was 7,949 miles.
	The first trial by jury in California is said
to have taken place, among a party of Mor-
mons at Yerba Buena, 1846.
	Dr. M'Cosh says that there are 20,000
Presbyterian congregations in the world,
embracing a population of 34,000,000.
	Iron boiler tubes in a steamship rust out
very fast when the vessel is laid by, they
endure five times longer when in constant
use.
	The large iron pipes employed in the
Glasgow (Scotland) Water-works were orig-
inally, four feet in diameter, 1 [-] of an inch
thick, and sustained a pressure from about
300 feet of head.
	A Major Colborne backs himself for £100
to walk fifteen miles in three hours on the
Maidstone road, England. He is to carry a
brick weighing six and a half pounds in
each hand, so as to touch his sides.
	Christopher Shearer, a fruit famer near
Reading, Pennsylvania, has probably the
largest refrigerator in the country. It is
fifty-five feet square, and holds, when filled,
about one hundred and forty cartloads of ice
and four thousand bushels of fruit.
	The Ipswich (England) "Journal" states
that on the 12th of May, at Lowestoft, Lady
Smith, aged 100 years, gave a dinner to 107
old people, whose united ages amounted to
8,228 years, or about an average of seventy-
seven to each.
	One-fourth of a share in a London water
company was lately sold for £16,000. The
face value of an entire share is only £1! It
is one of the wealthiest corporations in the
world, but its projector lost his fortune and 
died in the workouse. Its name is the
New River Company.

thoughts, we offer the following terms to clubs, so as
